self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

description: Self-contained Sadowsky input for the upper bound
full_text:
Below is a referee-usable way to replace the vague “Sadowsky input” in t7. I separate the **minimal external 3D placement lemma** from the **elementary cylinder-geometry checks**. This is the cleanest way to avoid hiding what is actually needed.

## 1. Minimal Sadowsky lemma needed

Let \(l>0\), \(d>0\), and define

\[
B(l,d)=\frac{l}{2\sqrt 3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

**Sadowsky three-cylinder lemma, exact form needed.**  
If \(0<b<B(l,d)\), then the Möbius rectangle

\[
M_{l,2b}=([0,l]\times[-b,b])/(0,t)\sim(l,-t)
\]

has an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric realization in \(\mathbb R^3\), built from three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces. The cylindrical pieces lie on rods of diameters

\[
2d,\quad d,\quad d,
\]

each cylindrical piece wrapping halfway around its rod. The midline projection is arranged on an equilateral triangle of side

\[
L=\frac l3,
\]

and the helical midline arcs meet the cylinder generators at angle \(60^\circ\). The seams between planar and cylindrical pieces are full-width generator segments, and adjacent pieces have the same tangent plane along each seam. Strict inequality \(b<B(l,d)\) gives embeddedness with positive clearance.

This is precisely Sadowsky’s three-rod construction: the translated original describes three rods, one diameter equal to the sum of the other two, producing three planar and three semicylindrical pieces; it also gives the equilateral-triangle projection, the \(60^\circ\) helical geometry, the length formulas, and the width bound below. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

The only genuinely external part in this lemma is the **cyclic 3D placement** of the three rods and tangent planes so that the six pieces close with one half-twist. Everything below is elementary once that placement is accepted.

---

## 2. Elementary length calculation

Let the three cylindrical midline arcs be

\[
AB,\qquad CD,\qquad EF,
\]

with \(AB\) on the cylinder of diameter \(2d\), and \(CD,EF\) on the cylinders of diameter \(d\).

For a cylinder of diameter \(D\), a half-wrap has circumferential distance

\[
\frac{\pi D}{2}.
\]

If the helical midline arc has length \(s\) and meets the generators at angle \(60^\circ\), then in the developed cylinder rectangle,

\[
s\sin 60^\circ=\frac{\pi D}{2}.
\]

Hence

\[
s=\frac{\pi D}{\sqrt3}.
\]

Its projection along the generator direction is

\[
s\cos 60^\circ=\frac{\pi D}{2\sqrt3}.
\]

Therefore, for the large cylinder \(D=2d\),

\[
AB=\frac{2\pi d}{\sqrt3},
\qquad
A'B'=\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3}.
\]

For each small cylinder \(D=d\),

\[
CD=EF=\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3},
\qquad
C'D'=E'F'=\frac{\pi d}{2\sqrt3}.
\]

These are exactly the formulas listed in Sadowsky’s construction. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

The straight planar midline pieces are \(BC,DE,FA\). The projected midline lies on the sides of an equilateral triangle of side \(L\). On two sides one subtracts one large-cylinder projected helical part and one small-cylinder projected helical part, so

\[
BC=FA
=
L-\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3}-\frac{\pi d}{2\sqrt3}
=
L-\frac{\sqrt3\pi d}{2}.
\]

On the remaining side one subtracts two small-cylinder projected helical parts, so

\[
DE
=
L-\frac{\pi d}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{\pi d}{2\sqrt3}
=
L-\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3}.
\]

Now the total midline length is

\[
\begin{aligned}
l_{\mathrm{mid}}
&=AB+BC+CD+DE+EF+FA\\
&=
\left(\frac{2\pi d}{\sqrt3}+\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3}+\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3}\right)
+
\left(2L-\sqrt3\pi d+L-\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3}\right)\\
&=
\frac{4\pi d}{\sqrt3}
+
3L-\frac{4\pi d}{\sqrt3}\\
&=3L.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus choosing

\[
L=\frac l3
\]

makes the constructed Möbius band have midline length exactly \(l\).

---

## 3. Width bound and positive clearance

In the flat development, the generator lines of the cylindrical parts meet the midline at \(60^\circ\). A full-width generator crosses from one boundary component to the other. If the half-width is \(b\), then the horizontal displacement from the midline to one boundary along such a generator is

\[
b\cot 60^\circ=\frac b{\sqrt3}.
\]

Thus the two boundary offsets from adjacent cylindrical pieces consume total midline length

\[
2b\cot60^\circ=\frac{2b}{\sqrt3}
\]

inside a planar connector. To avoid overlap, this must be no larger than the shortest planar midline connector. The shortest connector is

\[
BC=FA=L-\frac{\sqrt3\pi d}{2}.
\]

Therefore one needs

\[
\frac{2b}{\sqrt3}
\le
L-\frac{\sqrt3\pi d}{2}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
b
\le
\frac{\sqrt3 L}{2}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}
=
\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

This is Sadowsky’s width inequality. His construction states that at equality one may have boundary contact, while strict inequality prevents overlap; strictness therefore gives an open clearance margin. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

For our application, we need full width \(1\), hence half-width

\[
b=\frac12.
\]

Given \(\mu>\sqrt3\), choose \(d>0\) so small that

\[
\frac12
<
\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Equivalently, it suffices to choose

\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]

Then \(B(\mu,d)>1/2\), so Sadowsky’s construction gives an embedded width-one, length-\(\mu\) Möbius band.

---

## 4. The resulting lemma in the form needed by t7

**Lemma.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there exists an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), with positive clearance and with a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings.

**Proof.** Choose \(d\) satisfying

\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]

Set \(l=\mu\) and \(b=1/2\). Then

\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Apply the Sadowsky three-cylinder lemma above. The resulting band is built from three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces, has total midline length \(l=\mu\), full width \(2b=1\), and is embedded with positive clearance because the width inequality is strict. The planar pieces are flat isometric images of planar regions; the cylindrical pieces are standard isometric developments onto round cylinders; and adjacent pieces meet along tangent generator lines, so the whole surface is \(C^1\) and path-isometric. \(\square\)

---

## 5. Why this gives exactly what t7 uses

Let

\[
M_{\mu,1}=([0,\mu]\times[0,1])/(0,Y)\sim(\mu,1-Y).
\]

This is the same as the width-one strip above after translating the transverse coordinate \(t\in[-1/2,1/2]\) to \(Y=t+1/2\).

### Deck symmetry

Lift the Sadowsky map to

\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3.
\]

The deck transformation is

\[
H(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y),
\]

and, by construction on the quotient,

\[
E\circ H=E.
\]

Because the quotient Möbius band is embedded,

\[
E(P)=E(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

This is exactly the quotient-injectivity property required in t7 before the secant PL approximation.

### Full-width straight rulings

On each semicylindrical piece, the rulings are the cylinder generators. In the flat development they are straight full-width segments meeting the midline at \(60^\circ\). On each planar piece, one uses the corresponding straight full-width segments in the plane. Since the seams are themselves full-width generator segments, the foliations match continuously across seams.

Thus the lifted foliation may be written as

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),1)]
\]

for a continuous noncrossing endpoint function \(h\). Noncrossing implies \(h\) is strictly monotone, which is the property used in the invariant secant construction.

### \(C^1\) seams

Each planar piece is tangent to the adjacent cylinder along the shared generator line. A cylinder and its tangent plane have the same tangent plane along that generator. Therefore the first derivative of the surface map agrees from both sides. Curvature jumps across seams, so the surface is generally not \(C^2\), but it is \(C^1\), which is all the secant argument needs.

### Path isometry

On a planar piece, the map is a Euclidean rigid motion. On a cylindrical piece, the standard development

\[
(u,w)\mapsto p+u e+r\big(\cos(w/r)n+\sin(w/r)m\big)
\]

has first fundamental form

\[
du^2+dw^2.
\]

Hence each piece is intrinsically isometric to its flat preimage. Since pieces are glued along common full-width line segments by matching arclength, rectifiable curve lengths are preserved across the whole band. Therefore the Sadowsky band is path-isometric.

### Positive nonlocal separation

Strict width inequality gives no boundary contact between different threaded portions. Since the quotient band is compact and embedded, any two nonincident closed cells in the six-piece decomposition have positive Euclidean distance. More generally, for every intrinsic separation scale \(\eta>0\),

\[
\delta(\eta)
=
\min\{|E(p)-E(q)|:\operatorname{dist}_{M}(p,q)\ge \eta\}
>0.
\]

This is the “positive clearance” needed in t7: sufficiently fine secant approximations cannot create nonlocal intersections. Locally, \(C^1\) embeddedness gives graph neighborhoods, so fine secant facets also cannot fold through the surface near seams.

---

## 6. What remains external

The only part not expanded into coordinates here is the **closed 3D rod-and-tangent-plane placement**: namely, the Euclidean construction of three rods of diameters \(d,d,2d\), with tangent planar connectors, arranged so that the six pieces close with one half-twist. That is the minimal Sadowsky lemma. Once that placement is granted, all numerical formulas, the bound

\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4},
\]

the choice \(b=1/2,l=\mu\), the \(60^\circ\) helical lengths, path-isometry, \(C^1\) seams, full-width rulings, deck symmetry, and positive clearance follow by elementary cylinder geometry and compactness.

    {

      "is_original_problem": false,
      "sketch": "Choose l=mu, b=1/2, and d>0 with 1/2 < mu/(2 sqrt(3)) - 3 pi d/4; Sadowsky's three-cylinder construction gives the band, and the helical length and width inequalities follow from elementary 60-degree cylinder development geometry."
    },

      "sketch": "Deck symmetry and quotient-injectivity come from lifting an embedded quotient Moebius band; rulings are cylinder generators and planar straight segments; C1 seams come from tangency; path isometry comes from planar rigid motions and cylinder developments; positive separation follows from strict clearance and compact embeddedness."
    }
  ],

      "reason": "The cyclic placement of the three rods and tangent planes closing with one half-twist was isolated as the minimal external Sadowsky lemma rather than reconstructed in full coordinates."
    }
  ],
  "progress_notes": "The upper-bound proof can now cite a precise minimal Sadowsky input instead of a vague smooth-band black box. All parameter choices and all consequences needed for the PL secant construction have been derived explicitly from the Sadowsky geometry; only the classical 3D closure of the three-cylinder scaffold remains external."
}
</SOLVER_REPORT>

R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),1)]

for a continuous noncrossing endpoint function \(h\). Noncrossing implies \(h\) is strictly monotone, which is the property used in the invariant secant construction.

### \(C^1\) seams

Each planar piece is tangent to the adjacent cylinder along the shared generator line. A cylinder and its tangent plane have the same tangent plane along that generator. Therefore the first derivative of the surface map agrees from both sides. Curvature jumps across seams, so the surface is generally not \(C^2\), but it is \(C^1\), which is all the secant argument needs.

### Path isometry

On a planar piece, the map is a Euclidean rigid motion. On a cylindrical piece, the standard development

\[
(u,w)\mapsto p+u e+r\big(\cos(w/r)n+\sin(w/r)m\big)
\]

has first fundamental form

\[
du^2+dw^2.
\]

Hence each piece is intrinsically isometric to its flat preimage. Since pieces are glued along common full-width line segments by matching arclength, rectifiable curve lengths are preserved across the whole band. Therefore the Sadowsky band is path-isometric.

### Positive nonlocal separation

Strict width inequality gives no boundary contact between different threaded portions. Since the quotient band is compact and embedded, any two nonincident closed cells in the six-piece decomposition have positive Euclidean distance. More generally, for every intrinsic separation scale \(\eta>0\),

\[
\delta(\eta)
=
\min\{|E(p)-E(q)|:\operatorname{dist}_{M}(p,q)\ge \eta\}
>0.
\]

This is the “positive clearance” needed in t7: sufficiently fine secant approximations cannot create nonlocal intersections. Locally, \(C^1\) embeddedness gives graph neighborhoods, so fine secant facets also cannot fold through the surface near seams.

---

## 6. What remains external

The only part not expanded into coordinates here is the **closed 3D rod-and-tangent-plane placement**: namely, the Euclidean construction of three rods of diameters \(d,d,2d\), with tangent planar connectors, arranged so that the six pieces close with one half-twist. That is the minimal Sadowsky lemma. Once that placement is granted, all numerical formulas, the bound

\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4},
\]

the choice \(b=1/2,l=\mu\), the \(60^\circ\) helical lengths, path-isometry, \(C^1\) seams, full-width rulings, deck symmetry, and positive clearance follow by elementary cylinder geometry and compactness.

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

description: Skeptical referee check of the complete proof
full_text:
## Verdict

**No fatal gap found, provided the stated Sadowsky three-cylinder theorem is accepted as an input.** With that input, the lower-bound proof and the upper-bound construction can be made referee-tight, and the conclusion is:

\[
\{\beta:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty),
\qquad
\inf=\sqrt3.
\]

If the proof is required to be fully self-contained, then the upper bound is missing the proof of the Sadowsky construction; that is not a logical flaw in the argument after accepting the theorem, but it is an external dependency that must be cited or inserted.

---

## Fatal gaps if any

**None conditional on the Sadowsky input.**

The only potentially fatal issue is conditionality: the upper-bound proof invokes a classical Sadowsky three-cylinder construction in a fairly strong form: embedded, \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric, with positive clearance and a full-width straight ruling foliation for every length \(\mu>\sqrt3\). If that theorem is not allowed, the submitted proof is not self-contained. But once this theorem is accepted, I find no fatal gap.

---

## Repairable gaps if any

1. **Upper-bound embeddedness of the secant PL approximation needed more rigor.**  
   The informal “take \(\rho\) small” argument is correct but should be packaged as a lemma using the planar/cylindrical structure plus compactness and positive separation.

2. **Ruling-coordinate map \(h\) needed justification.**  
   One must state explicitly that a full-width ruling foliation of the lifted strip gives a continuous strictly increasing homeomorphism \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\). This follows from non-crossing and coverage of the two boundary lines.

3. **Leaf-graph connectedness upstairs/downstairs needs local-finiteness language.**  
   The open/closed argument is valid, but closedness of unions of infinitely many lifted triangles uses local finiteness.

4. **Metric endgame should use boundary arc lengths rather than a preferred minimal horizontal displacement.**  
   The \(t\)-based version in the lower-bound draft is the right one. It avoids hidden assumptions about choosing a lift with \(|s|<\beta\).

5. **Strict inequalities are safe but should be tracked.**  
   Lower bound: strictness comes from strict edge shortening/lengthening. Upper bound: boundary edges become strictly shortened because \(a<1\); transverse edges become strictly lengthened because \(\varepsilon\) is chosen below a positive anisotropic margin.

---

## Patched lemmas/proofs

### 1. Face-to-face refinement and cevian lengthening

For a clean triangle \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\), define

\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A((1-s)r_0+sr_1)|^2-|a-((1-s)r_0+sr_1)|^2 .
\]

The endpoint inequalities give \(q(0),q(1)>0\), and boundary shortening gives \(q''<0\). Hence \(q(s)>0\) for all \(s\in[0,1]\). Thus every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Nonboundary edges of a clean triangulation cannot cross transversely or partially overlap without forcing triangle interiors to overlap. Therefore the only non-face-to-face pathology is subdivision along boundary edges. Subdividing each boundary edge at all boundary vertices and coning to the opposite vertex preserves the squeeze inequalities by the cevian lemma. Local finiteness ensures only finitely many subdivisions per boundary edge.

### 2. Quotient finiteness and PL embedding

The quotient

\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle
\]

is a compact Möbius band with boundary length \(2\beta\). The orbit condition makes \(f\) descend to an injective continuous map \(F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3\), hence a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) at least distance \(\beta\). If \(f\) were non-injective on one triangle, affine degeneracy would give arbitrarily close distinct points with equal image, contradicting the orbit condition. Thus \(f\) is injective on each triangle. No triangle can meet a nontrivial translate of itself, so the quotient cellulation is regular. A compact fundamental domain meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, so the quotient triangulation is finite PL.

### 3. Leaf graph and orientation reversal

After face-to-face refinement, the cevian intervals in quotient triangles glue along nonboundary edges to form a finite 2-valent graph \(\Lambda_M\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is a union of circles. For any component \(C\), the union of triangles belonging to \(C\) is both open and closed: across nonboundary edges both adjacent triangles belong to the same component, and at boundary vertices the fan of incident triangles is connected through nonboundary edges. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected,

\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected by the same open/closed argument, using local finiteness. No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf: even powers translate both endpoints, while odd powers would force contradictory horizontal displacements. Therefore \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is the infinite cyclic cover, so \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\).

Orient lifted leaves from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Applying \(G_\beta\) swaps bottom and top, so after one deck step the image support line has the opposite orientation. Thus the oriented support-line path satisfies \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\).

### 4. Oriented-line Borsuk–Ulam lemma

The submitted proof of the oriented-line lemma is sound. Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) and \(m=p\times u\). Extend anti-periodically by \((u,m)(s+1)=-(u,m)(s)\). Define

\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]

\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]

The involution \((s,\delta)\mapsto(s+\delta,1-\delta)\) sends \((a,b)\) to \(-(a,b)\). If \((a,b)\) never vanished, its normalization would give a map from the cylinder to \(S^1\) whose middle circle has odd degree but is homotopic to a constant, contradiction. Thus two distinct support lines are perpendicular and coplanar, hence intersect.

### 5. Metric endgame

Let \(T,B\) be two quotient leaves whose image support lines are perpendicular and meet at \(P\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Choose \(B\) so that \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\). Let \(q\) be the farther endpoint of \(B\), \(Q=F(q)\). Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]

Thus \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and let \(D,H\) be the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\), with \(q\in D\). Write

\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

If a lift of \(T\) has horizontal displacement \(s\), then the boundary-coordinate relation gives

\[
s\equiv \pm t \pmod{2\beta},
\]

so \(|s|\ge |t|\). Therefore

\[
|F(T)|>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+s^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

Boundary shortening on \(H\) gives

\[
\beta-t>|F(T)|>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]

hence

\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t.
\]

For \(D\), the image boundary path goes from \(A\) to \(C\) through \(Q\). Since \(Q\) has distance \(y>1\) from the line \(AC\),

\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{|A-C|^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]

Boundary shortening gives

\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]

so

\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t.
\]

For all real \(t\),

\[
\max\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\sqrt{5+t^2}-t\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]

Therefore every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

### 6. Upper-bound secant PL approximation

Assuming the Sadowsky band \(E\) exists, the secant approximation is valid after the following formal patch.

The lifted ruling foliation gives rulings

\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)]
\]

with \(h\) a strictly increasing homeomorphism. The deck map satisfies

\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L,\qquad h(\phi(x))=x+L,\qquad \phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]

Thus \(|h(x)-x|\) is globally bounded.

Choose a fine partition \(x_0<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)\), include all planar/cylindrical seams, and extend by \(x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i)\). The quadrilaterals between consecutive rulings tile the strip. Since \(H\) swaps the two diagonal types, choosing diagonals anti-periodically gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation.

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by \(E\) and extend affinely. Embeddedness follows as follows:

- on planar pieces, the secant map equals \(E\);
- on cylindrical pieces, consecutive generators produce embedded chordal faces of an inscribed polygonal cylinder;
- across seams, the pieces meet \(C^1\), so sufficiently fine adjacent chordal faces are local graphs over the common tangent plane;
- nonlocal intersections are excluded by compact embeddedness and positive clearance of \(E\).

Hence the quotient PL map is embedded, and therefore

\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q).
\]

Boundary edges are nonexpanded because chords are no longer than the corresponding boundary arcs of the path-isometric surface. Ruling edges are exact. Diagonal edges lose at most \(\varepsilon\) by the triangle inequality, using the small top or bottom increments.

### 7. Anisotropic scaling margin

For given \(\beta>\sqrt3\), choose \(\mu\in(\sqrt3,\beta)\), set \(\lambda=\beta/\mu>1\), choose \(a<1\) close to \(1\) with \(a>1/\lambda\), and put

\[
c=a\lambda,\qquad L=a\beta=\mu c.
\]

The affine map

\[
A(x,y)=(ax,cy)
\]

conjugates \(G_\beta\) to the Sadowsky deck map \(H\).

For a transverse edge whose scaled horizontal span is \(t=cs\), \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), the scaled flat length is

\[
c\sqrt{s^2+1},
\]

while the original flat length is

\[
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]

At \(a=1\),

\[
\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}>\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
\]

uniformly on the compact interval \(0\le s\le D_\mu\). Therefore for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\), the margin remains positive. Choosing the secant error \(\varepsilon\) below this margin makes every transverse edge strictly lengthened. Boundary edges are strictly shortened because \(F_0\) is nonexpanding on boundary edges and \(A\) multiplies boundary lengths by \(a<1\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## Final checklist

| Issue | Status |
|---|---|
| Face-to-face cevian refinement | Valid |
| Quotient finiteness | Valid by local finiteness |
| PL embedding downstairs | Valid |
| Leaf graph is one circle | Valid after open/closed argument |
| Lifted leaf graph is \(\mathbb R\) | Valid; no deck stabilizers |
| Orientation reversal of support lines | Valid |
| Oriented-line Borsuk–Ulam lemma | Valid |
| Shared-endpoint metric cases | Valid; farther endpoint \(q\) avoids endpoints of \(T\) |
| Boundary-arc \(t\)-inequalities | Valid |
| Sadowsky input | External but sufficient |
| Ruling-coordinate \(h,\phi\) | Valid after explicit monotonicity/homeomorphism argument |
| Anti-periodic diagonals | Valid and necessary |
| Secant PL quotient-injectivity | Valid with patched embeddedness lemma |
| Boundary nonexpansion | Valid |
| Transverse length estimates | Valid |
| Anisotropic scaling margin | Valid and strict |

Therefore, with the Sadowsky theorem included or cited, the proof establishes exactly

\[
\{\beta:\beta\text{ realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]

and hence the desired infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

  "proven_results": [
    {
      "statement": "Assuming the stated Sadowsky three-cylinder existence theorem, the set of realized beta is exactly (sqrt(3), infinity), and therefore its infimum is sqrt(3).",
      "is_original_problem": true,
      "sketch": "The lower bound follows from cevian lengthening, a finite PL embedded quotient Mobius band, a leaf-circle support-line Borsuk-Ulam argument, and boundary-length inequalities forcing beta > sqrt(3). The upper bound uses Sadowsky's embedded ruled band for every mu > sqrt(3), an invariant secant PL approximation, and anisotropic pullback to strictly shorten boundary edges and strictly lengthen transverse edges."
    }
  ],
  "failed_attempts": [
    {
      "approach": "Treat the upper-bound proof as completely self-contained",
      "reason": "The construction relies on the external Sadowsky three-cylinder theorem; the rest of the proof is complete once that theorem is accepted or supplied."
    }
  ],
  "progress_notes": "No fatal gap was found conditional on the Sadowsky input. The main repairable point was making the secant PL embeddedness argument explicit; with the planar/cylindrical structure, C1 seam control, and positive clearance, this patch is valid."
}
</SOLVER_REPORT>

Boundary edges are nonexpanded because chords are no longer than the corresponding boundary arcs of the path-isometric surface. Ruling edges are exact. Diagonal edges lose at most \(\varepsilon\) by the triangle inequality, using the small top or bottom increments.

### 7. Anisotropic scaling margin

For given \(\beta>\sqrt3\), choose \(\mu\in(\sqrt3,\beta)\), set \(\lambda=\beta/\mu>1\), choose \(a<1\) close to \(1\) with \(a>1/\lambda\), and put

\[
c=a\lambda,\qquad L=a\beta=\mu c.
\]

The affine map

\[
A(x,y)=(ax,cy)
\]

conjugates \(G_\beta\) to the Sadowsky deck map \(H\).

For a transverse edge whose scaled horizontal span is \(t=cs\), \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), the scaled flat length is

\[
c\sqrt{s^2+1},
\]

while the original flat length is

\[
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]

At \(a=1\),

\[
\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}>\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
\]

uniformly on the compact interval \(0\le s\le D_\mu\). Therefore for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\), the margin remains positive. Choosing the secant error \(\varepsilon\) below this margin makes every transverse edge strictly lengthened. Boundary edges are strictly shortened because \(F_0\) is nonexpanding on boundary edges and \(A\) multiplies boundary lengths by \(a<1\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## Final checklist

| Issue | Status |
|---|---|
| Face-to-face cevian refinement | Valid |
| Quotient finiteness | Valid by local finiteness |
| PL embedding downstairs | Valid |
| Leaf graph is one circle | Valid after open/closed argument |
| Lifted leaf graph is \(\mathbb R\) | Valid; no deck stabilizers |
| Orientation reversal of support lines | Valid |
| Oriented-line Borsuk–Ulam lemma | Valid |
| Shared-endpoint metric cases | Valid; farther endpoint \(q\) avoids endpoints of \(T\) |
| Boundary-arc \(t\)-inequalities | Valid |
| Sadowsky input | External but sufficient |
| Ruling-coordinate \(h,\phi\) | Valid after explicit monotonicity/homeomorphism argument |
| Anti-periodic diagonals | Valid and necessary |
| Secant PL quotient-injectivity | Valid with patched embeddedness lemma |
| Boundary nonexpansion | Valid |
| Transverse length estimates | Valid |
| Anisotropic scaling margin | Valid and strict |

Therefore, with the Sadowsky theorem included or cited, the proof establishes exactly

\[
\{\beta:\beta\text{ realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]

and hence the desired infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

description: Standalone invariant secant PL approximation lemma
full_text:
Below is a standalone version of the secant approximation theorem, formulated with the extra geometric hypotheses actually needed for the embeddedness step.

## Theorem: invariant secant PL approximation of a Sadowsky-type ruled band

Let

\[
S_c=\mathbb R\times[0,c],\qquad H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]

with \(c,L>0\). Suppose \(E:S_c\to\mathbb R^3\) satisfies:

1. **Deck invariance and embedded quotient.**  
   \(E\circ H=E\), and \(E\) descends to an embedded \(C^1\) path-isometric Möbius band
   \[
   \bar E:S_c/\langle H\rangle\to\mathbb R^3.
   \]

2. **Deck-invariant full-width ruling foliation.**  
   The lifted rulings are exactly
   \[
   R_x=[B_x,T_x],\qquad B_x=(x,0),\quad T_x=(h(x),c),
   \]
   where \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous, the leaves \(R_x\) are pairwise disjoint and cover \(S_c\), and the foliation is preserved by \(H\). Also \(E(R_x)\) is a straight segment of the same length as \(R_x\).

3. **Adapted finite planar/cylindrical structure.**  
   Modulo \(H\), there are finitely many seam rulings. Between seam rulings, \(E\) is either an affine Euclidean isometry onto a plane, or a standard isometric development onto a round cylinder, with rulings sent to cylinder generators. Cylindrical pieces are simple generator strips over angular intervals of length at most \(\pi\). Adjacent pieces meet \(C^1\) along seam rulings.

Then \(h\) is a strictly increasing homeomorphism, the horizontal ruling span

\[
S:=\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|
\]

is finite, and the following holds.

For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists an \(H\)-invariant locally finite clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(S_c\), made from ruling strips and anti-periodic diagonals, and an \(H\)-invariant PL map

\[
F_0:S_c\to\mathbb R^3
\]

such that:

- \(F_0\) agrees with \(E\) on all vertices of \(\mathcal T_0\) and is affine on each triangle;
- \(F_0\) descends to an embedded PL Möbius band;
- equivalently,
  \[
  F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z;
  \]
- every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
- every nonboundary edge \(e\) satisfies
  \[
  |F_0(e)|\ge |e|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon;
  \]
- every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\). In particular, if \(\sigma(e)\) is its horizontal span, then
  \[
  |F_0(e)|\ge \sqrt{\sigma(e)^2+c^2}-\varepsilon,
  \qquad 0\le \sigma(e)<D.
  \]

---

## Proof

### 1. The ruling endpoint map \(h\)

For \(x_1<x_2\), write the horizontal coordinate of the leaf \(R_{x_j}\) at height \(Y\) as

\[
\ell_j(Y)=\left(1-\frac Yc\right)x_j+\frac Yc\,h(x_j).
\]

If \(h(x_1)>h(x_2)\), then \(\ell_2(0)-\ell_1(0)>0\) but \(\ell_2(c)-\ell_1(c)<0\), so the two leaves cross. Thus \(h(x_1)\le h(x_2)\). Equality would make the two leaves share the same top endpoint, contradicting the foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing.

Because the foliation covers the top boundary, every top point \((u,c)\) equals \(T_x=(h(x),c)\) for a unique \(x\). Thus \(h\) is onto. A continuous strictly increasing bijection \(\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is a homeomorphism.

---

### 2. The deck return map

Since \(H\) preserves the foliation,

\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)}
\]

for some increasing homeomorphism \(\phi\). But

\[
H(B_x)=(x+L,c),\qquad H(T_x)=(h(x)+L,0).
\]

Thus the bottom endpoint of \(H(R_x)\) is \((h(x)+L,0)\), so

\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]

Its top endpoint is \((x+L,c)\), hence

\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]

Therefore

\[
\phi^2(x)=\phi(\phi(x))=h(\phi(x))+L=x+2L.
\]

Also \(\phi(x)>x\). Indeed, \(\phi(x)=x\) is impossible because \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\), and if \(\phi(x)<x\), monotonicity gives \(\phi^2(x)<\phi(x)<x\), again impossible.

Finally,

\[
h(x+2L)=h(\phi^2(x))=\phi(x)+L=h(x)+2L,
\]

so \(h(x)-x\) is \(2L\)-periodic. Hence

\[
S=\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|<\infty.
\]

---

### 3. Choosing an invariant partition

Fix \(D>S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\). Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that

\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]

Choose \(x_0\in\mathbb R\). Since \(\phi(x_0)>x_0\), choose a finite partition

\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]

such that:

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]

all seam rulings in \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\) are included, and the partition is fine enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Extend the sequence by

\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]

Then

\[
x_{i+2N}=\phi^2(x_i)=x_i+2L.
\]

Also,

\[
h(x_{i+N})=h(\phi(x_i))=x_i+L.
\]

Thus

\[
x_{i+N+1}-x_{i+N}
=
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]

and

\[
h(x_{i+N+1})-h(x_{i+N})
=
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho.
\]

So the same small-increment estimates hold for every \(i\in\mathbb Z\). Since \(x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L\), the sequence is locally finite and tends to \(\pm\infty\).

Set

\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]

The consecutive ruling strips

\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}(B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i)
\]

tile \(S_c\). Indeed, at each height \(Y\), the horizontal coordinates of the ruling leaves form an increasing bi-infinite sequence tending to \(\pm\infty\).

---

### 4. Anti-periodic diagonal choice

There are two possible diagonals in \(Q_i\):

\[
d_i^+=B_iT_{i+1},\qquad d_i^-=B_{i+1}T_i.
\]

Using the deck relations,

\[
H(B_i)=T_{i+N},\qquad H(T_i)=B_{i+N}.
\]

Therefore

\[
H(d_i^+)=d_{i+N}^-,
\qquad
H(d_i^-)=d_{i+N}^+.
\]

So \(H\) swaps the two diagonal types. A uniform diagonal choice is not \(H\)-invariant.

Choose, for example,

\[
d_i=d_i^+\quad\text{for }0\le i<N,
\]

then impose

\[
d_{i+N}=H(d_i),
\qquad
d_{i+2N}=d_i.
\]

This gives an \(H\)-invariant locally finite clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\). Each triangle has all vertices on \(\partial S_c\), and exactly one edge contained in \(\partial S_c\).

---

### 5. The secant map

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by

\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]

and extend affinely over every triangle of \(\mathcal T_0\).

Because \(E\circ H=E\) and the triangulation is \(H\)-invariant,

\[
F_0\circ H=F_0.
\]

Thus \(F_0\) descends to a PL map on the quotient Möbius band.

---

### 6. Embeddedness of the PL quotient

We now justify that the partition can be chosen fine enough so that the quotient PL map is embedded.

On a planar piece, \(E\) is affine isometric, so the secant map agrees exactly with \(E\) there.

On a cylindrical piece, consecutive ruling generators determine planar chordal faces of an inscribed polygonal cylinder. Since each cylindrical piece is a simple strip over an angular interval of length at most \(\pi\), sufficiently fine chordal faces meet only along common generator edges. The radial projection back to the original cylinder is a homeomorphism on the chordal strip.

At a seam ruling, the two adjacent pieces share the same tangent plane along the seam. Since the quotient surface is \(C^1\), a neighborhood of the seam is a graph over that tangent plane, with the two sides projecting to opposite half-neighborhoods. For sufficiently small adjacent ruling intervals, the neighboring secant faces have planes arbitrarily close to the common tangent plane and project into the corresponding half-neighborhoods. Hence they meet only along the shared seam edge.

Boundary points are handled by the same one-sided graph argument.

Finally, nonlocal intersections are excluded by compactness and embeddedness of \(\bar E\). Choose finitely many local graph neighborhoods as above. The set of pairs of quotient points not contained in a common such neighborhood is compact and disjoint from the diagonal. Since \(\bar E\) is injective there, its image-pair distance has a positive minimum. The planar/cylindrical chordal approximations converge uniformly to \(\bar E\) as the angular and ruling mesh tends to zero, so for sufficiently fine mesh no new nonlocal intersections can appear.

Therefore the quotient PL map \(\bar F_0\) is embedded. Hence on the lift,

\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\iff
P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

---

### 7. Boundary edge estimates

Consider a bottom boundary edge \(B_iB_{i+1}\). Since \(E\) is path-isometric,

\[
\ell(E([B_i,B_{i+1}]))=x_{i+1}-x_i.
\]

Therefore the chord satisfies

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(B_{i+1})|
=
|E(B_i)-E(B_{i+1})|
\le x_{i+1}-x_i
=
|B_i-B_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Similarly,

\[
|F_0(T_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
=
|T_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Thus every boundary edge is nonexpanded.

---

### 8. Ruling edge estimates

For a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\), the image \(E(R_{x_i})\) is a straight segment of the same length as \(R_{x_i}\). Hence

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|
=
|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|
=
|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

So ruling edges are exact.

---

### 9. Diagonal edge estimates

First consider a plus diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let

\[
r_i=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}},
\qquad
d_i^+=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

The map \(u\mapsto \sqrt{u^2+c^2}\) is \(1\)-Lipschitz, so

\[
|d_i^+-r_i|
\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
<\rho.
\]

Also, by the boundary estimate on the top edge,

\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|
\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
<\rho.
\]

Thus

\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=
|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r_i-\rho\\
&\ge d_i^+-2\rho\\
&>d_i^+-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]

For a minus diagonal \(B_{i+1}T_i\), the same argument uses the bottom boundary edge:

\[
|E(B_i)-E(B_{i+1})|\le x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,
\]

and

\[
\bigl||B_{i+1}-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}-|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}\bigr|
\le x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho.
\]

Therefore every diagonal edge also loses less than \(\varepsilon\).

Consequently every nonboundary edge \(e\) satisfies

\[
|F_0(e)|\ge |e|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
\]

---

### 10. Horizontal span bound

A ruling edge \(B_iT_i\) has horizontal span

\[
|h(x_i)-x_i|\le S<D.
\]

A plus diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span

\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
<S+\rho<D.
\]

A minus diagonal \(B_{i+1}T_i\) has horizontal span

\[
|h(x_i)-x_{i+1}|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+x_{i+1}-x_i
<S+\rho<D.
\]

Thus every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\). Since every nonboundary edge joins the two boundary components, if its horizontal span is \(\sigma(e)\), then

\[
|e|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{\sigma(e)^2+c^2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|F_0(e)|\ge \sqrt{\sigma(e)^2+c^2}-\varepsilon,
\qquad 0\le \sigma(e)<D.
\]

This is the precise estimate needed for the later anisotropic scaling step.

---

## Extra hypotheses actually needed

Beyond mere \(H\)-invariance, path-isometry, and a noncrossing ruling foliation, the embeddedness proof uses:

1. finitely many planar/cylindrical pieces modulo \(H\);
2. seam rulings included in the partition;
3. \(C^1\) tangency across seams;
4. cylindrical pieces that are simple generator strips, in the Sadowsky case semicylinders;
5. positive nonlocal clearance, which follows from compact embeddedness once local graph neighborhoods are fixed.

Without these structural assumptions, boundary and edge-length estimates still make sense, but embeddedness of the secant PL quotient would require a different approximation theorem.

<SOLVER_REPORT>
{
  "proven_results": [
    {
      "statement": "Under the stated Sadowsky-type planar/cylindrical, C1 seam, clearance, and full-width ruling hypotheses, for every D greater than the maximal horizontal ruling span and every epsilon > 0 there is an H-invariant locally finite clean secant triangulation with anti-periodic diagonals and an H-invariant affine vertex-secant map F0 whose quotient is embedded, whose boundary edges are nonexpanded, and whose nonboundary edges lose at most epsilon in length and have horizontal span < D.",
      "is_original_problem": false,
      "sketch": "The ruling foliation forces h to be a strictly increasing homeomorphism and gives the deck return map phi(x)=h(x)+L with phi^2(x)=x+2L. A fine partition of [x0,phi(x0)] is extended by phi, diagonals are chosen anti-periodically because H swaps the two diagonal types, and embeddedness follows from exact planar pieces, embedded chordal polygonal-cylinder pieces, C1 seam graph neighborhoods, and compact nonlocal clearance."
    }
  ],
  "failed_attempts": [
    {
      "approach": "C0 closeness alone",
      "reason": "The clean triangles are full-width and do not have small flat diameter, so embeddedness cannot be justified by uniform closeness alone; the planar/cylindrical ruled structure and seam graph argument are needed."
    }
  ],
  "progress_notes": "The secant PL approximation theorem needed for the Sadowsky upper bound is now formalized. The only remaining external input for the full upper bound is the existence of the Sadowsky-type embedded planar/cylindrical Möbius band satisfying these hypotheses."
}
</SOLVER_REPORT>
solution:
Below is a standalone version of the secant approximation theorem, formulated with the extra geometric hypotheses actually needed for the embeddedness step.

## Theorem: invariant secant PL approximation of a Sadowsky-type ruled band

Let

\[
S_c=\mathbb R\times[0,c],\qquad H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]

with \(c,L>0\). Suppose \(E:S_c\to\mathbb R^3\) satisfies:

1. **Deck invariance and embedded quotient.**  
   \(E\circ H=E\), and \(E\) descends to an embedded \(C^1\) path-isometric Möbius band
   \[
   \bar E:S_c/\langle H\rangle\to\mathbb R^3.
   \]

2. **Deck-invariant full-width ruling foliation.**  
   The lifted rulings are exactly
   \[
   R_x=[B_x,T_x],\qquad B_x=(x,0),\quad T_x=(h(x),c),
   \]
   where \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous, the leaves \(R_x\) are pairwise disjoint and cover \(S_c\), and the foliation is preserved by \(H\). Also \(E(R_x)\) is a straight segment of the same length as \(R_x\).

3. **Adapted finite planar/cylindrical structure.**  
   Modulo \(H\), there are finitely many seam rulings. Between seam rulings, \(E\) is either an affine Euclidean isometry onto a plane, or a standard isometric development onto a round cylinder, with rulings sent to cylinder generators. Cylindrical pieces are simple generator strips over angular intervals of length at most \(\pi\). Adjacent pieces meet \(C^1\) along seam rulings.

Then \(h\) is a strictly increasing homeomorphism, the horizontal ruling span

\[
S:=\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|
\]

is finite, and the following holds.

For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists an \(H\)-invariant locally finite clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(S_c\), made from ruling strips and anti-periodic diagonals, and an \(H\)-invariant PL map

\[
F_0:S_c\to\mathbb R^3
\]

such that:

- \(F_0\) agrees with \(E\) on all vertices of \(\mathcal T_0\) and is affine on each triangle;
- \(F_0\) descends to an embedded PL Möbius band;
- equivalently,
  \[
  F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z;
  \]
- every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
- every nonboundary edge \(e\) satisfies
  \[
  |F_0(e)|\ge |e|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon;
  \]
- every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\). In particular, if \(\sigma(e)\) is its horizontal span, then
  \[
  |F_0(e)|\ge \sqrt{\sigma(e)^2+c^2}-\varepsilon,
  \qquad 0\le \sigma(e)<D.
  \]

---

## Proof

### 1. The ruling endpoint map \(h\)

For \(x_1<x_2\), write the horizontal coordinate of the leaf \(R_{x_j}\) at height \(Y\) as

\[
\ell_j(Y)=\left(1-\frac Yc\right)x_j+\frac Yc\,h(x_j).
\]

If \(h(x_1)>h(x_2)\), then \(\ell_2(0)-\ell_1(0)>0\) but \(\ell_2(c)-\ell_1(c)<0\), so the two leaves cross. Thus \(h(x_1)\le h(x_2)\). Equality would make the two leaves share the same top endpoint, contradicting the foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing.

Because the foliation covers the top boundary, every top point \((u,c)\) equals \(T_x=(h(x),c)\) for a unique \(x\). Thus \(h\) is onto. A continuous strictly increasing bijection \(\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is a homeomorphism.

---

### 2. The deck return map

Since \(H\) preserves the foliation,

\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)}
\]

for some increasing homeomorphism \(\phi\). But

\[
H(B_x)=(x+L,c),\qquad H(T_x)=(h(x)+L,0).
\]

Thus the bottom endpoint of \(H(R_x)\) is \((h(x)+L,0)\), so

\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]

Its top endpoint is \((x+L,c)\), hence

\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]

Therefore

\[
\phi^2(x)=\phi(\phi(x))=h(\phi(x))+L=x+2L.
\]

Also \(\phi(x)>x\). Indeed, \(\phi(x)=x\) is impossible because \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\), and if \(\phi(x)<x\), monotonicity gives \(\phi^2(x)<\phi(x)<x\), again impossible.

Finally,

\[
h(x+2L)=h(\phi^2(x))=\phi(x)+L=h(x)+2L,
\]

so \(h(x)-x\) is \(2L\)-periodic. Hence

\[
S=\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|<\infty.
\]

---

### 3. Choosing an invariant partition

Fix \(D>S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\). Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that

\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]

Choose \(x_0\in\mathbb R\). Since \(\phi(x_0)>x_0\), choose a finite partition

\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]

such that:

\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]

all seam rulings in \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\) are included, and the partition is fine enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Extend the sequence by

\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]

Then

\[
x_{i+2N}=\phi^2(x_i)=x_i+2L.
\]

Also,

\[
h(x_{i+N})=h(\phi(x_i))=x_i+L.
\]

Thus

\[
x_{i+N+1}-x_{i+N}
=
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]

and

\[
h(x_{i+N+1})-h(x_{i+N})
=
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho.
\]

So the same small-increment estimates hold for every \(i\in\mathbb Z\). Since \(x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L\), the sequence is locally finite and tends to \(\pm\infty\).

Set

\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]

The consecutive ruling strips

\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}(B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i)
\]

tile \(S_c\). Indeed, at each height \(Y\), the horizontal coordinates of the ruling leaves form an increasing bi-infinite sequence tending to \(\pm\infty\).

---

### 4. Anti-periodic diagonal choice

There are two possible diagonals in \(Q_i\):

\[
d_i^+=B_iT_{i+1},\qquad d_i^-=B_{i+1}T_i.
\]

Using the deck relations,

\[
H(B_i)=T_{i+N},\qquad H(T_i)=B_{i+N}.
\]

Therefore

\[
H(d_i^+)=d_{i+N}^-,
\qquad
H(d_i^-)=d_{i+N}^+.
\]

So \(H\) swaps the two diagonal types. A uniform diagonal choice is not \(H\)-invariant.

Choose, for example,

\[
d_i=d_i^+\quad\text{for }0\le i<N,
\]

then impose

\[
d_{i+N}=H(d_i),
\qquad
d_{i+2N}=d_i.
\]

This gives an \(H\)-invariant locally finite clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\). Each triangle has all vertices on \(\partial S_c\), and exactly one edge contained in \(\partial S_c\).

---

### 5. The secant map

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by

\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]

and extend affinely over every triangle of \(\mathcal T_0\).

Because \(E\circ H=E\) and the triangulation is \(H\)-invariant,

\[
F_0\circ H=F_0.
\]

Thus \(F_0\) descends to a PL map on the quotient Möbius band.

---

### 6. Embeddedness of the PL quotient

We now justify that the partition can be chosen fine enough so that the quotient PL map is embedded.

On a planar piece, \(E\) is affine isometric, so the secant map agrees exactly with \(E\) there.

On a cylindrical piece, consecutive ruling generators determine planar chordal faces of an inscribed polygonal cylinder. Since each cylindrical piece is a simple strip over an angular interval of length at most \(\pi\), sufficiently fine chordal faces meet only along common generator edges. The radial projection back to the original cylinder is a homeomorphism on the chordal strip.

At a seam ruling, the two adjacent pieces share the same tangent plane along the seam. Since the quotient surface is \(C^1\), a neighborhood of the seam is a graph over that tangent plane, with the two sides projecting to opposite half-neighborhoods. For sufficiently small adjacent ruling intervals, the neighboring secant faces have planes arbitrarily close to the common tangent plane and project into the corresponding half-neighborhoods. Hence they meet only along the shared seam edge.

Boundary points are handled by the same one-sided graph argument.

Finally, nonlocal intersections are excluded by compactness and embeddedness of \(\bar E\). Choose finitely many local graph neighborhoods as above. The set of pairs of quotient points not contained in a common such neighborhood is compact and disjoint from the diagonal. Since \(\bar E\) is injective there, its image-pair distance has a positive minimum. The planar/cylindrical chordal approximations converge uniformly to \(\bar E\) as the angular and ruling mesh tends to zero, so for sufficiently fine mesh no new nonlocal intersections can appear.

Therefore the quotient PL map \(\bar F_0\) is embedded. Hence on the lift,

\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\iff
P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

---

### 7. Boundary edge estimates

Consider a bottom boundary edge \(B_iB_{i+1}\). Since \(E\) is path-isometric,

\[
\ell(E([B_i,B_{i+1}]))=x_{i+1}-x_i.
\]

Therefore the chord satisfies

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(B_{i+1})|
=
|E(B_i)-E(B_{i+1})|
\le x_{i+1}-x_i
=
|B_i-B_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Similarly,

\[
|F_0(T_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
=
|T_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Thus every boundary edge is nonexpanded.

---

### 8. Ruling edge estimates

For a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\), the image \(E(R_{x_i})\) is a straight segment of the same length as \(R_{x_i}\). Hence

\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|
=
|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|
=
|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

So ruling edges are exact.

---

### 9. Diagonal edge estimates

First consider a plus diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let

\[
r_i=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}},
\qquad
d_i^+=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

The map \(u\mapsto \sqrt{u^2+c^2}\) is \(1\)-Lipschitz, so

\[
|d_i^+-r_i|
\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
<\rho.
\]

Also, by the boundary estimate on the top edge,

\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|
\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
<\rho.
\]

Thus

\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=
|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r_i-\rho\\
&\ge d_i^+-2\rho\\
&>d_i^+-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]

For a minus diagonal \(B_{i+1}T_i\), the same argument uses the bottom boundary edge:

\[
|E(B_i)-E(B_{i+1})|\le x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,
\]

and

\[
\bigl||B_{i+1}-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}-|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}\bigr|
\le x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho.
\]

Therefore every diagonal edge also loses less than \(\varepsilon\).

Consequently every nonboundary edge \(e\) satisfies

\[
|F_0(e)|\ge |e|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
\]

---

### 10. Horizontal span bound

A ruling edge \(B_iT_i\) has horizontal span

\[
|h(x_i)-x_i|\le S<D.
\]

A plus diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span

\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
<S+\rho<D.
\]

A minus diagonal \(B_{i+1}T_i\) has horizontal span

\[
|h(x_i)-x_{i+1}|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+x_{i+1}-x_i
<S+\rho<D.
\]

Thus every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\). Since every nonboundary edge joins the two boundary components, if its horizontal span is \(\sigma(e)\), then

\[
|e|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{\sigma(e)^2+c^2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|F_0(e)|\ge \sqrt{\sigma(e)^2+c^2}-\varepsilon,
\qquad 0\le \sigma(e)<D.
\]

This is the precise estimate needed for the later anisotropic scaling step.

---

## Extra hypotheses actually needed

Beyond mere \(H\)-invariance, path-isometry, and a noncrossing ruling foliation, the embeddedness proof uses:

1. finitely many planar/cylindrical pieces modulo \(H\);
2. seam rulings included in the partition;
3. \(C^1\) tangency across seams;
4. cylindrical pieces that are simple generator strips, in the Sadowsky case semicylinders;
5. positive nonlocal clearance, which follows from compact embeddedness once local graph neighborhoods are fixed.

Without these structural assumptions, boundary and edge-length estimates still make sense, but embeddedness of the secant PL quotient would require a different approximation theorem.

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

description: Attempt direct finite PL upper construction without Sadowsky
full_text:
Yes — the Sadowsky input can be avoided for the **upper bound**. A \(9\)-vertex resolved version of the collapsed \(3\)-triangle model is enough.

Below, all indices are mod \(9\).

## Direct construction for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Set
\[
B=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(L<B\). Choose a number
\[
L<\sigma<B
\]
and let
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L,\;B-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose \(0<\lambda<\min(\Delta/20,\beta/12)\).

### 1. The clean \(G_\beta\)-invariant triangulation

Define boundary edge lengths periodically by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
B-2\lambda, & j\equiv 2\pmod 3,\\
\lambda, & j\not\equiv 2\pmod 3.
\end{cases}
\]
Then over one period,
\[
\sum_{j=0}^8\ell_j=3B=2\beta.
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), \(c_{j+1}=c_j+\ell_j\), and extend by \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\).

Define bottom and top vertices in \(\Sigma\) by
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
The sequences \(X_j\) and \(Y_j\) are strictly increasing along the two boundary lines. The quadrilaterals
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}
\]
tile \(\Sigma\). Split each \(Q_j\) by the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), giving two clean triangles
\[
D_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j\},
\]
\[
E_j=\operatorname{conv}\{Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}\}.
\]
This is locally finite and clean. Moreover
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5},
\]
so
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
Thus the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

The quotient has \(9\) boundary vertices \(v_0,\dots,v_8\), boundary edges \(v_jv_{j+1}\), and faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]
Its interior edges are \(e_j=v_jv_{j+5}\). This is the standard \(9\)-triangle clean triangulation of a Möbius band.

---

## 2. Target vertices in \(\mathbb R^3\)

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in the plane \(z=0\), and let \(o\) be its centroid. For scale \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+z\,e_3,\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]

Choose very small \(\eta,h>0\). Put
\[
A=(1,0),\qquad B'=(1-\eta,h),\qquad C=(1+\eta,2h),
\]
where the first coordinate is scale and the second is height. Define
\[
q_i^A=q_i(1,0),\qquad q_i^{B'}=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad q_i^C=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]

Now define nine target boundary vertices:
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0^C&q_0^{B'}&q_0^A&
q_1^A&q_1^C&q_1^{B'}&
q_2^{B'}&q_2^A&q_2^C.
\end{array}
\]

For \(\eta,h\) sufficiently small:

* every distance between vertices based at different \(p_i\)’s lies in
  \[
  (\sigma-\Delta/4,\ \sigma+\Delta/4);
  \]
* every same-cluster boundary distance
  \[
  |P_0P_1|,\ |P_1P_2|,\ |P_3P_4|,\ |P_4P_5|,\ |P_6P_7|,\ |P_7P_8|
  \]
  is \(<\lambda/2\).

This follows by continuity from the collapsed \(\eta=h=0\) model.

Define the quotient PL map by sending \(v_j\mapsto P_j\), and each face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5})
\]
affinely to the triangle
\[
F_j=\operatorname{conv}\{P_j,P_{j+1},P_{j+5}\}.
\]

---

## 3. Embeddedness of the target Möbius band

The nine target triangles decompose as follows:

* three horizontal triangles:
  \[
  F_2=[q_0^A,q_1^A,q_2^A],
  \]
  \[
  F_5=[q_1^{B'},q_2^{B'},q_0^{B'}],
  \]
  \[
  F_8=[q_2^C,q_0^C,q_1^C];
  \]

* three side quadrilaterals, each split into two triangles:
  \[
  F_0\cup F_4 \quad\text{connects side } p_0p_1 \text{ between levels } B',C,
  \]
  \[
  F_3\cup F_7 \quad\text{connects side } p_1p_2 \text{ between levels } A,C,
  \]
  \[
  F_1\cup F_6 \quad\text{connects side } p_2p_0 \text{ between levels } A,B'.
  \]

A horizontal slice proves embeddedness. For \(0<z<h\), the slice consists of two side segments of homothetic equilateral triangles with distinct scales
\[
1+\eta\frac{z}{2h}
\quad\text{and}\quad
1-\eta\frac{z}{h}.
\]
Distinct-scale adjacent sides of concentric homothetic triangles are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), the slice again consists of two side segments with distinct scales
\[
1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}
\quad\text{and}\quad
1+\eta\frac{z}{2h},
\]
which are unequal except at the prescribed endpoint \(z=2h\).

At \(z=h\), the extra side segment from the \(A\)-to-\(C\) panel has scale \(1+\eta/2\), while the middle triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), so that segment lies outside the middle triangle. Thus the only intersections are the intended shared edges and vertices. Therefore the quotient PL map is embedded.

---

## 4. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

For \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), the boundary edge length in the flat Möbius band is \(\ell_j=\lambda\), while its target length is \(<\lambda/2\).

For \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge length is
\[
\ell_j=B-2\lambda,
\]
while its target length is \(<\sigma+\Delta/4\). Since \(\sigma\le B-\Delta\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le B-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<B-2\lambda.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

---

## 5. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Consider the quotient interior edge
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
In the chosen lift its horizontal span is
\[
d_j-\beta,\qquad d_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
With the periodic pattern \(\lambda,\lambda,B-2\lambda\), one gets
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
B+2\lambda, & j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2B-\lambda, & j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge length is
\[
<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]

On the target side, every interior edge joins two different clusters, so its length is
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\sigma\ge L+\Delta\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore each affine face map is a squeeze map.

---

## 6. Lift and orbit condition

Define \(f\) on the strip vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). Since
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5},
\]
and \(P_{j+9}=P_j\), we have
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]
Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\) on the Möbius band. Consequently
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
\bar p=\bar q
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

So every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized by this explicit clean PL construction, with no Sadowsky or smooth paper-band input.

---

The naive \(3\)-vertex model fails because all three faces map to the same equilateral triangle. A purely vertical stacking of three copies also has a median-level crossing. The small homothetic scale perturbation \(1,\ 1-\eta,\ 1+\eta\) is the key extra move that resolves the crossing while preserving all strict length margins.

<SOLVER_REPORT>
{
  "proven_results": [
    {
      "statement": "For every beta > sqrt(3), beta is realized by an explicit 9-periodic clean PL construction, without invoking Sadowsky or any smooth paper-band theorem.",
      "is_original_problem": false,
      "sketch": "Use a 9-vertex quotient clean triangulation with boundary lengths lambda, lambda, 2beta/3-2lambda repeated. Map its vertices to three tiny clusters around an equilateral triangle of side sigma between sqrt(1+beta^2/9) and 2beta/3, using three nearby height/scale levels to obtain an embedded PL Mobius band; boundary edges shorten and all transverse edges lengthen by the chosen margins."
    }
  ],
  "failed_attempts": [
    {
      "approach": "Unresolved 3-boundary-vertex model",
      "reason": "It gives the correct edge-length inequalities but maps all quotient faces to the same geometric triangle, so the quotient map is not injective."
    },
    {
      "approach": "Pure vertical stacking of three identical triangular sheets",
      "reason": "One side panel necessarily crosses the middle horizontal triangle; adding a tiny homothetic scale perturbation removes this median-level intersection."
    }
  ],
  "progress_notes": "The Sadowsky black box is eliminated for the upper bound. Combining this direct construction with the already established lower-bound argument gives the infimum sqrt(3)."
}
</SOLVER_REPORT>

  \]
  \[
  F_5=[q_1^{B'},q_2^{B'},q_0^{B'}],
  \]
  \[
  F_8=[q_2^C,q_0^C,q_1^C];
  \]

* three side quadrilaterals, each split into two triangles:
  \[
  F_0\cup F_4 \quad\text{connects side } p_0p_1 \text{ between levels } B',C,
  \]
  \[
  F_3\cup F_7 \quad\text{connects side } p_1p_2 \text{ between levels } A,C,
  \]
  \[
  F_1\cup F_6 \quad\text{connects side } p_2p_0 \text{ between levels } A,B'.
  \]

A horizontal slice proves embeddedness. For \(0<z<h\), the slice consists of two side segments of homothetic equilateral triangles with distinct scales
\[
1+\eta\frac{z}{2h}
\quad\text{and}\quad
1-\eta\frac{z}{h}.
\]
Distinct-scale adjacent sides of concentric homothetic triangles are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), the slice again consists of two side segments with distinct scales
\[
1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}
\quad\text{and}\quad
1+\eta\frac{z}{2h},
\]
which are unequal except at the prescribed endpoint \(z=2h\).

At \(z=h\), the extra side segment from the \(A\)-to-\(C\) panel has scale \(1+\eta/2\), while the middle triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), so that segment lies outside the middle triangle. Thus the only intersections are the intended shared edges and vertices. Therefore the quotient PL map is embedded.

---

## 4. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

For \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), the boundary edge length in the flat Möbius band is \(\ell_j=\lambda\), while its target length is \(<\lambda/2\).

For \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge length is
\[
\ell_j=B-2\lambda,
\]
while its target length is \(<\sigma+\Delta/4\). Since \(\sigma\le B-\Delta\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le B-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<B-2\lambda.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

---

## 5. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Consider the quotient interior edge
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
In the chosen lift its horizontal span is
\[
d_j-\beta,\qquad d_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
With the periodic pattern \(\lambda,\lambda,B-2\lambda\), one gets
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
B+2\lambda, & j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2B-\lambda, & j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge length is
\[
<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]

On the target side, every interior edge joins two different clusters, so its length is
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\sigma\ge L+\Delta\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore each affine face map is a squeeze map.

---

## 6. Lift and orbit condition

Define \(f\) on the strip vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). Since
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5},
\]
and \(P_{j+9}=P_j\), we have
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]
Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\) on the Möbius band. Consequently
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
\bar p=\bar q
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

So every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized by this explicit clean PL construction, with no Sadowsky or smooth paper-band input.

---

The naive \(3\)-vertex model fails because all three faces map to the same equilateral triangle. A purely vertical stacking of three copies also has a median-level crossing. The small homothetic scale perturbation \(1,\ 1-\eta,\ 1+\eta\) is the key extra move that resolves the crossing while preserving all strict length margins.

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
