self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

description: Referee-ready explicit 9-periodic upper construction
full_text:
## Theorem: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct explicitly a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\) and a PL map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) satisfying the two realization conditions.

### 1. Constants

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\),
\[
L<W.
\]
Choose, explicitly,
\[
\sigma=\frac{L+W}{2},\qquad \Delta=\sigma-L=W-\sigma=\frac{W-L}{2}>0,
\]
and
\[
\lambda=\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{40},\frac{\beta}{24}\right\}.
\]
Thus
\[
0<\lambda<\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{20},\frac{\beta}{12}\right\}.
\]

Define a \(3\)-periodic sequence of positive boundary lengths by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Every block of three consecutive \(\ell_j\)’s has sum \(W\), hence every block of nine has sum \(3W=2\beta\).

Let \(c_0=0\) and define
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j,\qquad j\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

---

### 2. The clean triangulation of the strip

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Because \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\), we have
\[
X_{j+9}=X_j+(2\beta,0),\qquad Y_{j+9}=Y_j+(2\beta,0).
\]

Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(t\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(t)=(1-t)c_j+t(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(t)=(1-t)c_{j+1}+t(c_{j+6}-\beta).
\]
Their difference is
\[
x_{j+1}(t)-x_j(t)
=(1-t)\ell_j+t\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus for each fixed \(t\), the intervals
\[
[x_j(t),x_{j+1}(t)]
\]
tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{t\}\), consecutively and without overlap of interiors. Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\) as \(j\to\pm\infty\), these intervals cover the whole line and the family is locally finite. Therefore the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), and define clean triangles
\[
D_j=[X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j],
\qquad
E_j=[Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}].
\]
The collection
\[
\mathcal T=\{D_j,E_j:j\in\mathbb Z\}
\]
is a countable, locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Now check \(G_\beta\)-invariance. Since
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y),
\]
we have
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because
\[
Y_{j+4}=(c_{j+9}-\beta,1)=(c_j+\beta,1),
\]
and also
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So \(\mathcal T\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) denote the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), the indices \(j\) are taken modulo \(9\) in the quotient. Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\), because \(G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}\). Hence
\[
D_j\mapsto T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],
\]
and \(E_j\) maps to the same list of quotient faces, shifted. Thus the quotient has nine triangular faces
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],
\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

---

### 3. The target nine-vertex PL Möbius band

Choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\subset\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). Let
\[
R=|p_i-o|=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3}.
\]

Choose small positive parameters explicitly by
\[
\eta=\frac12\min\left\{\frac12,\frac{\lambda}{8R},\frac{\Delta}{16R}\right\},
\qquad
h=\frac12\min\left\{\frac{\lambda}{8},\frac{\Delta}{16}\right\}.
\]
Thus \(0<\eta<1\), and
\[
\eta R\le \frac{\lambda}{16},\qquad h\le\frac{\lambda}{16},
\]
and also
\[
\eta R\le \frac{\Delta}{32},\qquad h\le\frac{\Delta}{32}.
\]

For \(r>0\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Now set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad
M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad
C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define the nine target vertices \(P_j\) by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
Extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map \(F\) by
\[
F(v_j)=P_j,
\]
affine on each triangle
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}].
\]
The nine target faces are therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

---

### 4. Embeddedness of the target surface

Let \(H(r,z)\) be the homothetic horizontal triangle
\[
H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}.
\]
Let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) be the side of \(H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\).

Use barycentric coordinates \((\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\) with respect to \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) in each horizontal plane. For
\[
a(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]
one has
\[
H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge a(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\},
\]
and
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=a(r)\}\cap H(r,z),
\qquad \{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}.
\]
Hence adjacent side segments \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only at their common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The three horizontal triangles are
\[
F_2=H(1,0),
\qquad
F_5=H(1-\eta,h),
\qquad
F_8=H(1+\eta,2h).
\]

The remaining six faces form three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
\Pi_{01}=F_0\cup F_4,\qquad
\Pi_{12}=F_3\cup F_7,\qquad
\Pi_{20}=F_1\cup F_6.
\]
Their horizontal sections are:
\[
\Pi_{01}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad h\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
\[
\Pi_{12}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
and
\[
\Pi_{20}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le h.
\]

Now check slices.

For \(0<\zeta<h\), only \(\Pi_{12}\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
\[
1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}>1,
\qquad
1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h}<1,
\]
so the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<\zeta<2h\), only \(\Pi_{01}\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) occur. Their scales satisfy
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac{\zeta}{h}\right)>0,
\]
so again the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(\zeta=0\), the bottom triangle \(H(1,0)\) meets \(\Pi_{12}\) along \(A_1A_2\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) along \(A_2A_0\), exactly as prescribed.

At \(\zeta=h\), the middle triangle \(H(1-\eta,h)\) meets \(\Pi_{01}\) along \(M_0M_1\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) along \(M_2M_0\). The extra slice of \(\Pi_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), which is larger than \(1-\eta\), so it is disjoint from the middle triangle and from the other two side slices.

At \(\zeta=2h\), the top triangle \(H(1+\eta,2h)\) meets \(\Pi_{01}\) along \(C_0C_1\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) along \(C_1C_2\), exactly as prescribed.

Each \(\Pi_{ij}\) is a planar convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles inside a given panel meet only along that diagonal. The slice analysis shows that distinct panels and horizontal triangles have no unintended intersections. Therefore the nine target triangles meet exactly according to the quotient complex. Hence \(F\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

---

### 5. Target distance estimates

Because of the explicit choices of \(\eta,h\), if \(U_i,V_i\in\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\) are in the same cluster, then
\[
|U_iV_i|\le 2\eta R+2h\le \frac{\lambda}{4}<\frac{\lambda}{2}.
\]
If \(i\ne k\), and \(U_i\in\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\), \(V_k\in\{A_k,M_k,C_k\}\), then \(U_iV_k\) differs from the original base edge \(p_ip_k\) by a perturbation of norm at most \(2\eta R+2h\le \Delta/8\). Therefore
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}
<
|U_iV_k|
<
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]

The cluster labels of \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) are
\[
0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2.
\]
Thus boundary edges \(P_jP_{j+1}\) with \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\) lie inside one cluster, while those with \(j\equiv2\pmod3\) join different clusters.

---

### 6. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

The flat boundary edge corresponding to \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=\lambda,
\]
and the target edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) lies inside one cluster, so
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\frac{\lambda}{2}<\lambda=\ell_j.
\]

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=W-2\lambda.
\]
The target edge joins different clusters, so
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(W=\sigma+\Delta\) and \(2\lambda\le \Delta/20\),
\[
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}
=
W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}
<
W-2\lambda
=
\ell_j.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

---

### 7. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Consider the nonboundary edge \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|^2=1+(d_j-\beta)^2.
\]
A direct computation from the \(\ell_j\)-pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\), this means
\[
d_j-\beta=
\begin{cases}
-\beta/3+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
\beta/3-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Using \(\lambda<\beta/12\), we get
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]

The other nonboundary edge in \(D_j\), namely \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), satisfies
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}
=
-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so the same estimate gives
\[
|X_{j+1}Y_j|<L.
\]
The same two edge types occur in the \(E_j\)’s. Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length \(<L\).

Every target nonboundary edge is of the form \(P_kP_{k+5}\) for some \(k\), hence joins different clusters. Therefore
\[
|P_kP_{k+5}|>\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
But \(\sigma=L+\Delta\), so
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}
=
L+\frac{3\Delta}{4}
>
L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

---

### 8. Define the lifted map \(f\) and verify the deck-orbit condition

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,
\qquad
f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with indices on \(P\) taken modulo \(9\), and extend affinely over every \(D_j\) and \(E_j\).

The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous and PL. Also,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since both sides are affine on each triangle and \(G_\beta\) sends triangles to triangles, we get
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\).

Because \(F\) is injective on the quotient Möbius band,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
F(\pi(p))=F(\pi(q))
\iff
\pi(p)=\pi(q)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Finally, by the boundary and nonboundary edge estimates above, the affine restriction of \(f\) to every clean triangle is a squeeze map: its boundary edge is strictly shortened, and its two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened.

Therefore every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. No indexing correction is needed; the key shifts are
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5},
\qquad
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

solution:
## Theorem: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct explicitly a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\) and a PL map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) satisfying the two realization conditions.

### 1. Constants

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\),
\[
L<W.
\]
Choose, explicitly,
\[
\sigma=\frac{L+W}{2},\qquad \Delta=\sigma-L=W-\sigma=\frac{W-L}{2}>0,
\]
and
\[
\lambda=\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{40},\frac{\beta}{24}\right\}.
\]
Thus
\[
0<\lambda<\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{20},\frac{\beta}{12}\right\}.
\]

Define a \(3\)-periodic sequence of positive boundary lengths by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Every block of three consecutive \(\ell_j\)’s has sum \(W\), hence every block of nine has sum \(3W=2\beta\).

Let \(c_0=0\) and define
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j,\qquad j\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

---

### 2. The clean triangulation of the strip

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Because \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\), we have
\[
X_{j+9}=X_j+(2\beta,0),\qquad Y_{j+9}=Y_j+(2\beta,0).
\]

Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(t\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(t)=(1-t)c_j+t(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(t)=(1-t)c_{j+1}+t(c_{j+6}-\beta).
\]
Their difference is
\[
x_{j+1}(t)-x_j(t)
=(1-t)\ell_j+t\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus for each fixed \(t\), the intervals
\[
[x_j(t),x_{j+1}(t)]
\]
tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{t\}\), consecutively and without overlap of interiors. Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\) as \(j\to\pm\infty\), these intervals cover the whole line and the family is locally finite. Therefore the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), and define clean triangles
\[
D_j=[X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j],
\qquad
E_j=[Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}].
\]
The collection
\[
\mathcal T=\{D_j,E_j:j\in\mathbb Z\}
\]
is a countable, locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Now check \(G_\beta\)-invariance. Since
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y),
\]
we have
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because
\[
Y_{j+4}=(c_{j+9}-\beta,1)=(c_j+\beta,1),
\]
and also
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So \(\mathcal T\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) denote the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), the indices \(j\) are taken modulo \(9\) in the quotient. Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\), because \(G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}\). Hence
\[
D_j\mapsto T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],
\]
and \(E_j\) maps to the same list of quotient faces, shifted. Thus the quotient has nine triangular faces
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],
\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

---

### 3. The target nine-vertex PL Möbius band

Choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\subset\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). Let
\[
R=|p_i-o|=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3}.
\]

Choose small positive parameters explicitly by
\[
\eta=\frac12\min\left\{\frac12,\frac{\lambda}{8R},\frac{\Delta}{16R}\right\},
\qquad
h=\frac12\min\left\{\frac{\lambda}{8},\frac{\Delta}{16}\right\}.
\]
Thus \(0<\eta<1\), and
\[
\eta R\le \frac{\lambda}{16},\qquad h\le\frac{\lambda}{16},
\]
and also
\[
\eta R\le \frac{\Delta}{32},\qquad h\le\frac{\Delta}{32}.
\]

For \(r>0\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Now set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad
M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad
C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define the nine target vertices \(P_j\) by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
Extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map \(F\) by
\[
F(v_j)=P_j,
\]
affine on each triangle
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}].
\]
The nine target faces are therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

---

### 4. Embeddedness of the target surface

Let \(H(r,z)\) be the homothetic horizontal triangle
\[
H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}.
\]
Let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) be the side of \(H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\).

Use barycentric coordinates \((\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\) with respect to \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) in each horizontal plane. For
\[
a(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]
one has
\[
H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge a(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\},
\]
and
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=a(r)\}\cap H(r,z),
\qquad \{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}.
\]
Hence adjacent side segments \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only at their common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The three horizontal triangles are
\[
F_2=H(1,0),
\qquad
F_5=H(1-\eta,h),
\qquad
F_8=H(1+\eta,2h).
\]

The remaining six faces form three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
\Pi_{01}=F_0\cup F_4,\qquad
\Pi_{12}=F_3\cup F_7,\qquad
\Pi_{20}=F_1\cup F_6.
\]
Their horizontal sections are:
\[
\Pi_{01}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad h\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
\[
\Pi_{12}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
and
\[
\Pi_{20}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le h.
\]

Now check slices.

For \(0<\zeta<h\), only \(\Pi_{12}\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
\[
1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}>1,
\qquad
1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h}<1,
\]
so the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<\zeta<2h\), only \(\Pi_{01}\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) occur. Their scales satisfy
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac{\zeta}{h}\right)>0,
\]
so again the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(\zeta=0\), the bottom triangle \(H(1,0)\) meets \(\Pi_{12}\) along \(A_1A_2\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) along \(A_2A_0\), exactly as prescribed.

At \(\zeta=h\), the middle triangle \(H(1-\eta,h)\) meets \(\Pi_{01}\) along \(M_0M_1\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) along \(M_2M_0\). The extra slice of \(\Pi_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), which is larger than \(1-\eta\), so it is disjoint from the middle triangle and from the other two side slices.

At \(\zeta=2h\), the top triangle \(H(1+\eta,2h)\) meets \(\Pi_{01}\) along \(C_0C_1\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) along \(C_1C_2\), exactly as prescribed.

Each \(\Pi_{ij}\) is a planar convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles inside a given panel meet only along that diagonal. The slice analysis shows that distinct panels and horizontal triangles have no unintended intersections. Therefore the nine target triangles meet exactly according to the quotient complex. Hence \(F\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

---

### 5. Target distance estimates

Because of the explicit choices of \(\eta,h\), if \(U_i,V_i\in\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\) are in the same cluster, then
\[
|U_iV_i|\le 2\eta R+2h\le \frac{\lambda}{4}<\frac{\lambda}{2}.
\]
If \(i\ne k\), and \(U_i\in\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\), \(V_k\in\{A_k,M_k,C_k\}\), then \(U_iV_k\) differs from the original base edge \(p_ip_k\) by a perturbation of norm at most \(2\eta R+2h\le \Delta/8\). Therefore
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}
<
|U_iV_k|
<
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]

The cluster labels of \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) are
\[
0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2.
\]
Thus boundary edges \(P_jP_{j+1}\) with \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\) lie inside one cluster, while those with \(j\equiv2\pmod3\) join different clusters.

---

### 6. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

The flat boundary edge corresponding to \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=\lambda,
\]
and the target edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) lies inside one cluster, so
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\frac{\lambda}{2}<\lambda=\ell_j.
\]

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=W-2\lambda.
\]
The target edge joins different clusters, so
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(W=\sigma+\Delta\) and \(2\lambda\le \Delta/20\),
\[
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}
=
W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}
<
W-2\lambda
=
\ell_j.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

---

### 7. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Consider the nonboundary edge \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|^2=1+(d_j-\beta)^2.
\]
A direct computation from the \(\ell_j\)-pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\), this means
\[
d_j-\beta=
\begin{cases}
-\beta/3+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
\beta/3-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Using \(\lambda<\beta/12\), we get
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]

The other nonboundary edge in \(D_j\), namely \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), satisfies
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}
=
-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so the same estimate gives
\[
|X_{j+1}Y_j|<L.
\]
The same two edge types occur in the \(E_j\)’s. Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length \(<L\).

Every target nonboundary edge is of the form \(P_kP_{k+5}\) for some \(k\), hence joins different clusters. Therefore
\[
|P_kP_{k+5}|>\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
But \(\sigma=L+\Delta\), so
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}
=
L+\frac{3\Delta}{4}
>
L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

---

### 8. Define the lifted map \(f\) and verify the deck-orbit condition

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,
\qquad
f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with indices on \(P\) taken modulo \(9\), and extend affinely over every \(D_j\) and \(E_j\).

The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous and PL. Also,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since both sides are affine on each triangle and \(G_\beta\) sends triangles to triangles, we get
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\).

Because \(F\) is injective on the quotient Möbius band,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
F(\pi(p))=F(\pi(q))
\iff
\pi(p)=\pi(q)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Finally, by the boundary and nonboundary edge estimates above, the affine restriction of \(f\) to every clean triangle is a squeeze map: its boundary edge is strictly shortened, and its two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened.

Therefore every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. No indexing correction is needed; the key shifts are
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5},
\qquad
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

description: writeup (full problem): For the original problem, prove that √3 is the infimum of the set of realized numbers; ideally prove the stronger statement that the realized numbers are exactly (√3, ∞).
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following stronger statement. Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers, \(\mathbb R\) the reals, let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and for \(\beta>0\) let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\), with \(G_\beta^k\) denoting the \(k\)-fold iterate for \(k\in\mathbb Z\). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\); a clean triangulation is a countable locally finite covering of \(\Sigma\) by clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors; it is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the triangle’s boundary edge and strictly lengthens its other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) that is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies \(f(p)=f(q)\) if and only if \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Then the set of realized numbers is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
In particular, its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This is stronger than the original requested infimum statement, and is not a relaxation.

## Proof

We prove the two inclusions separately.

---

# Part I. Lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized by \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

## 1. Cevian lengthening and a face-to-face refinement

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\), and let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave and lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Thus every cevian from the apex \(a\) to the boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened.

We next make the triangulation face-to-face. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: near a transverse crossing, the interiors of the two incident triangles would occupy two open half-planes with nonparallel boundary lines, and these open half-planes overlap in a sector. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then, since each joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), they have the same endpoints and are the same edge. Thus the only possible non-face-to-face behavior occurs along boundary edges.

For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on that edge, and cone the subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies only finitely many such subdivision points occur on each boundary edge. The cevian argument above shows that every new nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened, and every new boundary subedge is strictly shortened because \(f\) is affine on the original boundary edge. The refined triangulation is still clean, locally finite, \(G_\beta\)-invariant, and squeeze-compatible. We use this refinement from now on.

## 2. Quotient Möbius band and finite PL embedding

Let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
Equivalently, \(M\) is obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying \((0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y)\). Thus \(M\) is a flat Möbius band whose boundary has length \(2\beta\).

The orbit condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous injective map
\[
F:M\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed, even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), and odd powers move \(x\) by an odd multiple of \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were not injective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), giving arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), impossible when \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

No closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\); injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles by local finiteness. Therefore the quotient cellulation of \(M\) has finitely many triangles. Thus \(F\) is a finite PL embedding, affine on every quotient triangle. Also, because each boundary edge is strictly shortened and \(F\) is affine on boundary edges, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in path length.

## 3. The cevian leaf graph

In each clean triangle, take the family of cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. This family is an interval; its endpoints correspond to the two nonboundary side edges. Glue two such interval endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary edges are the same edge of the quotient cellulation. The result is a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), the leaf graph.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence each component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of the triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the adjacent triangle belongs to the same component. At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges except at the two extreme boundary edges. Hence all triangles incident to a given boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M\). Thus
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same argument upstairs shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The natural map
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering. No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf: if a leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even deck power translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd deck power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Therefore the covering has infinite fibers, and since it is connected,
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient each lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By the cevian lengthening lemma, its image is a nondegenerate line segment, so it has an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented support lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) is oriented from \(G_\beta t\) to \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment has the opposite orientation. Therefore, along one deck period of the quotient leaf circle, the oriented support-line path satisfies
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

## 4. Oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma

We need the following elementary lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) with \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then two distinct parameter values modulo \(1\) give support lines that are perpendicular and intersect.

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) and extend them to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)
\]
satisfies
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

If \((a,b)\) never vanished, then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
would define a map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map satisfying this antipodal relation has odd degree, while it is homotopic through \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to a constant map, contradiction. Hence \(a=b=0\) for some \(0<\delta<1\).

The equation \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the equation \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. This proves the lemma.

Applying the lemma to the support-line path gives two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

## 5. Winding control for leaves

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf, oriented from bottom to top, with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Its flat length is \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\). We claim
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]

If \(t=\beta\), the endpoints are identified by \(G_\beta\), so their images under \(f\) coincide, contradicting the positive image length of \(J\). The case \(t=-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\).

If \(t>\beta\), then \(J\) intersects \(G_\beta J\) in relative interiors. After translating \(x\) to \(0\), the segments are
\[
(0,0)\to(t,1)
\]
and
\[
(t+\beta,0)\to(\beta,1),
\]
and they meet at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate image segment \(f(J)\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Hence \(|t|<\beta\).

Parametrize the quotient boundary by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\): a top point \((u,1)\) corresponds to bottom coordinate \(u-\beta\). Thus the two boundary endpoints of \(J\) have boundary coordinates \(x\) and \(x+t-\beta\). Since \(|t|<\beta\), the two boundary arcs between them have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]
Replacing \(J\) by \(G_\beta J\) changes \(t\) to \(-t\).

## 6. Metric endgame

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the two quotient leaves whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the leaves so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) is contained in a closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By cevian lengthening,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1,
\]
so
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), forcing \(y=0\).

Let \(a,c\in\partial M\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and write
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1),
\]
and, replacing the lift by its \(G_\beta\)-translate if necessary, arrange that the boundary arc \(D\) from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc, so
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
By cevian lengthening,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), strict boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
and hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Thus
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then the elementary projection estimate gives
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting \(Q\) orthogonally to the line \(AC\), the left side is minimized when the projection is the midpoint of \(AC\). Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
and hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), contradiction. Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

# Part II. Upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Put
\[
b=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L_*=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), one has \(L_*<b\). Choose
\[
L_*<\sigma<b,
\]
and define
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_*,\,b-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

## 1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation

Define positive boundary lengths periodically by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
b-2\lambda, & j\equiv 2\pmod 3,\\
\lambda, & j\not\equiv 2\pmod 3.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{8}\ell_j=3b=2\beta.
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), \(c_{j+1}=c_j+\ell_j\), so \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\). Define
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
The points \(X_j\) are strictly ordered on the bottom boundary, and the points \(Y_j\) are strictly ordered on the top boundary.

Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
The quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\): at height \(y\), their vertical sides occur at
\[
(1-y)c_j+y(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
a strictly increasing sequence tending to \(\pm\infty\). Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), giving clean triangles
\[
D_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j\},
\]
\[
E_j=\operatorname{conv}\{Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}\}.
\]
This is a locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Moreover,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5},
\]
so
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
Hence the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient Möbius band, let \(v_j\) be the boundary vertex represented by \(X_j\), with indices read modulo \(9\). The quotient has boundary edges \(v_jv_{j+1}\) and faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

## 2. Target vertices in \(\mathbb R^3\)

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in the plane \(z=0\), and let \(o\) be its centroid. Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). For scale \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+z e_3,\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]
Choose small \(\eta,h>0\), to be fixed below, and write
\[
q_i^A=q_i(1,0),\qquad
q_i^M=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad
q_i^C=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
As \(\eta,h\to0\), all three points \(q_i^A,q_i^M,q_i^C\) converge to \(p_i\). Therefore we may choose \(\eta,h>0\) so small that:

1. If \(i\neq k\) and \(U,V\in\{A,M,C\}\), then
   \[
   \sigma-\Delta/4<|q_i^U-q_k^V|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
   \]
2. If \(i=k\) and \(U,V\in\{A,M,C\}\), then
   \[
   |q_i^U-q_i^V|<\lambda/2.
   \]

Define nine target boundary vertices, with indices modulo \(9\), by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0^C&q_0^M&q_0^A&
q_1^A&q_1^C&q_1^M&
q_2^M&q_2^A&q_2^C .
\end{array}
\]
Define the quotient PL map by sending \(v_j\mapsto P_j\) and mapping each face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5})
\]
affinely to
\[
F_j=\operatorname{conv}\{P_j,P_{j+1},P_{j+5}\}.
\]

## 3. Embeddedness of the quotient PL map

The nine target triangles decompose as follows.

The faces \(F_2,F_5,F_8\) are horizontal triangles:
\[
F_2=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0^A,q_1^A,q_2^A\},
\]
\[
F_5=\operatorname{conv}\{q_1^M,q_2^M,q_0^M\},
\]
\[
F_8=\operatorname{conv}\{q_2^C,q_0^C,q_1^C\}.
\]

The remaining six faces form three side panels:
\[
F_1\cup F_6
\]
connects the side \(p_2p_0\) between levels \(A\) and \(M\);
\[
F_3\cup F_7
\]
connects the side \(p_1p_2\) between levels \(A\) and \(C\);
\[
F_0\cup F_4
\]
connects the side \(p_0p_1\) between levels \(M\) and \(C\).

At height \(0<z<h\), the surface slice consists of two side segments: the \(p_2p_0\)-side of the homothetic triangle of scale
\[
1-\eta z/h,
\]
and the \(p_1p_2\)-side of the homothetic triangle of scale
\[
1+\eta z/(2h).
\]
These are adjacent sides of concentric homothetic equilateral triangles with different scales, hence are disjoint.

At height \(h<z<2h\), the slice consists of the \(p_0p_1\)-side of scale
\[
1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h,
\]
and the \(p_1p_2\)-side of scale
\[
1+\eta z/(2h).
\]
These scales are unequal for \(h<z<2h\), so the two adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=h\), the middle horizontal triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), while the extra \(p_1p_2\)-side coming from the \(A\)-to-\(C\) panel has scale \(1+\eta/2\), hence lies outside the middle triangle. At \(z=0\) and \(z=2h\), the side panels meet the horizontal triangles exactly along the prescribed boundary sides and vertices.

Thus the only intersections among the nine target triangles are the common faces prescribed by the quotient cellulation. Hence the quotient PL map is an embedding.

## 4. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

For \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda.
\]

For \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge has length
\[
b-2\lambda.
\]
The target endpoints lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le b-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le b-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<b-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

## 5. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Let
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
A lift of \(e_j\) is the segment from \(X_j\) to \(Y_j\), whose horizontal span is
\[
s_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta)-c_j=d_j-\beta,
\]
where
\[
d_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
From the periodic pattern,
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
b+2\lambda, & j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2b-\lambda, & j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(\beta=3b/2\), we get
\[
|s_j|<\beta/3.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length
\[
\sqrt{1+s_j^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_*.
\]

On the target side, \(P_j\) and \(P_{j+5}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+5}|>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_*\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L_*.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Consequently each affine face map is a squeeze map.

## 6. Lift and orbit-injectivity

Define \(f\) on the strip vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). Because \(P_{j+9}=P_j\),
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) maps triangles affinely to triangles, this gives
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient PL map constructed above.

Therefore, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) and \(q\) have the same image in the quotient \(\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), equivalently if and only if
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining Part I and Part II, the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). ∎

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following stronger statement. Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers, \(\mathbb R\) the reals, let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and for \(\beta>0\) let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\), with \(G_\beta^k\) denoting the \(k\)-fold iterate for \(k\in\mathbb Z\). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\); a clean triangulation is a countable locally finite covering of \(\Sigma\) by clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors; it is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the triangle’s boundary edge and strictly lengthens its other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) that is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies \(f(p)=f(q)\) if and only if \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Then the set of realized numbers is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
In particular, its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This is stronger than the original requested infimum statement, and is not a relaxation.

## Proof

We prove the two inclusions separately.

---

# Part I. Lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized by \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

## 1. Cevian lengthening and a face-to-face refinement

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\), and let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave and lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Thus every cevian from the apex \(a\) to the boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened.

We next make the triangulation face-to-face. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: near a transverse crossing, the interiors of the two incident triangles would occupy two open half-planes with nonparallel boundary lines, and these open half-planes overlap in a sector. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then, since each joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), they have the same endpoints and are the same edge. Thus the only possible non-face-to-face behavior occurs along boundary edges.

For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on that edge, and cone the subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies only finitely many such subdivision points occur on each boundary edge. The cevian argument above shows that every new nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened, and every new boundary subedge is strictly shortened because \(f\) is affine on the original boundary edge. The refined triangulation is still clean, locally finite, \(G_\beta\)-invariant, and squeeze-compatible. We use this refinement from now on.

## 2. Quotient Möbius band and finite PL embedding

Let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
Equivalently, \(M\) is obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying \((0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y)\). Thus \(M\) is a flat Möbius band whose boundary has length \(2\beta\).

The orbit condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous injective map
\[
F:M\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed, even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), and odd powers move \(x\) by an odd multiple of \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were not injective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), giving arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), impossible when \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

No closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\); injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles by local finiteness. Therefore the quotient cellulation of \(M\) has finitely many triangles. Thus \(F\) is a finite PL embedding, affine on every quotient triangle. Also, because each boundary edge is strictly shortened and \(F\) is affine on boundary edges, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in path length.

## 3. The cevian leaf graph

In each clean triangle, take the family of cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. This family is an interval; its endpoints correspond to the two nonboundary side edges. Glue two such interval endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary edges are the same edge of the quotient cellulation. The result is a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), the leaf graph.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence each component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of the triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the adjacent triangle belongs to the same component. At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges except at the two extreme boundary edges. Hence all triangles incident to a given boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M\). Thus
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same argument upstairs shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The natural map
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering. No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf: if a leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even deck power translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd deck power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Therefore the covering has infinite fibers, and since it is connected,
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient each lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By the cevian lengthening lemma, its image is a nondegenerate line segment, so it has an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented support lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) is oriented from \(G_\beta t\) to \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment has the opposite orientation. Therefore, along one deck period of the quotient leaf circle, the oriented support-line path satisfies
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

## 4. Oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma

We need the following elementary lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) with \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then two distinct parameter values modulo \(1\) give support lines that are perpendicular and intersect.

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) and extend them to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)
\]
satisfies
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

If \((a,b)\) never vanished, then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
would define a map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map satisfying this antipodal relation has odd degree, while it is homotopic through \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to a constant map, contradiction. Hence \(a=b=0\) for some \(0<\delta<1\).

The equation \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the equation \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. This proves the lemma.

Applying the lemma to the support-line path gives two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

## 5. Winding control for leaves

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf, oriented from bottom to top, with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Its flat length is \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\). We claim
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]

If \(t=\beta\), the endpoints are identified by \(G_\beta\), so their images under \(f\) coincide, contradicting the positive image length of \(J\). The case \(t=-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\).

If \(t>\beta\), then \(J\) intersects \(G_\beta J\) in relative interiors. After translating \(x\) to \(0\), the segments are
\[
(0,0)\to(t,1)
\]
and
\[
(t+\beta,0)\to(\beta,1),
\]
and they meet at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate image segment \(f(J)\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Hence \(|t|<\beta\).

Parametrize the quotient boundary by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\): a top point \((u,1)\) corresponds to bottom coordinate \(u-\beta\). Thus the two boundary endpoints of \(J\) have boundary coordinates \(x\) and \(x+t-\beta\). Since \(|t|<\beta\), the two boundary arcs between them have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]
Replacing \(J\) by \(G_\beta J\) changes \(t\) to \(-t\).

## 6. Metric endgame

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the two quotient leaves whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the leaves so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) is contained in a closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By cevian lengthening,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1,
\]
so
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), forcing \(y=0\).

Let \(a,c\in\partial M\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and write
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1),
\]
and, replacing the lift by its \(G_\beta\)-translate if necessary, arrange that the boundary arc \(D\) from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc, so
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
By cevian lengthening,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), strict boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
and hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Thus
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then the elementary projection estimate gives
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting \(Q\) orthogonally to the line \(AC\), the left side is minimized when the projection is the midpoint of \(AC\). Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
and hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), contradiction. Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

# Part II. Upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Put
\[
b=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L_*=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), one has \(L_*<b\). Choose
\[
L_*<\sigma<b,
\]
and define
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_*,\,b-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

## 1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation

Define positive boundary lengths periodically by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
b-2\lambda, & j\equiv 2\pmod 3,\\
\lambda, & j\not\equiv 2\pmod 3.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{8}\ell_j=3b=2\beta.
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), \(c_{j+1}=c_j+\ell_j\), so \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\). Define
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
The points \(X_j\) are strictly ordered on the bottom boundary, and the points \(Y_j\) are strictly ordered on the top boundary.

Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
The quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\): at height \(y\), their vertical sides occur at
\[
(1-y)c_j+y(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
a strictly increasing sequence tending to \(\pm\infty\). Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), giving clean triangles
\[
D_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j\},
\]
\[
E_j=\operatorname{conv}\{Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}\}.
\]
This is a locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Moreover,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5},
\]
so
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
Hence the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient Möbius band, let \(v_j\) be the boundary vertex represented by \(X_j\), with indices read modulo \(9\). The quotient has boundary edges \(v_jv_{j+1}\) and faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

## 2. Target vertices in \(\mathbb R^3\)

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in the plane \(z=0\), and let \(o\) be its centroid. Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). For scale \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+z e_3,\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]
Choose small \(\eta,h>0\), to be fixed below, and write
\[
q_i^A=q_i(1,0),\qquad
q_i^M=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad
q_i^C=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
As \(\eta,h\to0\), all three points \(q_i^A,q_i^M,q_i^C\) converge to \(p_i\). Therefore we may choose \(\eta,h>0\) so small that:

1. If \(i\neq k\) and \(U,V\in\{A,M,C\}\), then
   \[
   \sigma-\Delta/4<|q_i^U-q_k^V|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
   \]
2. If \(i=k\) and \(U,V\in\{A,M,C\}\), then
   \[
   |q_i^U-q_i^V|<\lambda/2.
   \]

Define nine target boundary vertices, with indices modulo \(9\), by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0^C&q_0^M&q_0^A&
q_1^A&q_1^C&q_1^M&
q_2^M&q_2^A&q_2^C .
\end{array}
\]
Define the quotient PL map by sending \(v_j\mapsto P_j\) and mapping each face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5})
\]
affinely to
\[
F_j=\operatorname{conv}\{P_j,P_{j+1},P_{j+5}\}.
\]

## 3. Embeddedness of the quotient PL map

The nine target triangles decompose as follows.

The faces \(F_2,F_5,F_8\) are horizontal triangles:
\[
F_2=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0^A,q_1^A,q_2^A\},
\]
\[
F_5=\operatorname{conv}\{q_1^M,q_2^M,q_0^M\},
\]
\[
F_8=\operatorname{conv}\{q_2^C,q_0^C,q_1^C\}.
\]

The remaining six faces form three side panels:
\[
F_1\cup F_6
\]
connects the side \(p_2p_0\) between levels \(A\) and \(M\);
\[
F_3\cup F_7
\]
connects the side \(p_1p_2\) between levels \(A\) and \(C\);
\[
F_0\cup F_4
\]
connects the side \(p_0p_1\) between levels \(M\) and \(C\).

At height \(0<z<h\), the surface slice consists of two side segments: the \(p_2p_0\)-side of the homothetic triangle of scale
\[
1-\eta z/h,
\]
and the \(p_1p_2\)-side of the homothetic triangle of scale
\[
1+\eta z/(2h).
\]
These are adjacent sides of concentric homothetic equilateral triangles with different scales, hence are disjoint.

At height \(h<z<2h\), the slice consists of the \(p_0p_1\)-side of scale
\[
1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h,
\]
and the \(p_1p_2\)-side of scale
\[
1+\eta z/(2h).
\]
These scales are unequal for \(h<z<2h\), so the two adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=h\), the middle horizontal triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), while the extra \(p_1p_2\)-side coming from the \(A\)-to-\(C\) panel has scale \(1+\eta/2\), hence lies outside the middle triangle. At \(z=0\) and \(z=2h\), the side panels meet the horizontal triangles exactly along the prescribed boundary sides and vertices.

Thus the only intersections among the nine target triangles are the common faces prescribed by the quotient cellulation. Hence the quotient PL map is an embedding.

## 4. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

For \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda.
\]

For \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge has length
\[
b-2\lambda.
\]
The target endpoints lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le b-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le b-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<b-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

## 5. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Let
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
A lift of \(e_j\) is the segment from \(X_j\) to \(Y_j\), whose horizontal span is
\[
s_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta)-c_j=d_j-\beta,
\]
where
\[
d_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
From the periodic pattern,
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
b+2\lambda, & j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2b-\lambda, & j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(\beta=3b/2\), we get
\[
|s_j|<\beta/3.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length
\[
\sqrt{1+s_j^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_*.
\]

On the target side, \(P_j\) and \(P_{j+5}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+5}|>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_*\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L_*.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Consequently each affine face map is a squeeze map.

## 6. Lift and orbit-injectivity

Define \(f\) on the strip vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). Because \(P_{j+9}=P_j\),
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) maps triangles affinely to triangles, this gives
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient PL map constructed above.

Therefore, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) and \(q\) have the same image in the quotient \(\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), equivalently if and only if
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining Part I and Part II, the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). ∎

With clean triangles, clean triangulations, squeeze maps, and realized numbers defined exactly as in the original problem, the set of realized \(\beta>0\) is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\). In particular, the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).
