description: writeup (full problem): For Σ = R × [0,1], Gβ(x,y) = (x+β,1−y), and realized numbers as defined in the original problem, the realized set is exactly (√3, ∞). In particular, the infimum of the realized numbers is √3.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

For every real number \(\beta>0\), let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), let \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\). For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the usual \(k\)-fold iterate, using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) when \(k<0\) and the identity when \(k=0\). A clean triangle means the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\); its unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) is called its boundary edge. A clean triangulation means a countable, locally finite collection of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\). It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle means an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the boundary edge and strictly lengthens the two other edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) which is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies
\[
f(p)=f(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z
\]
for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\).

The proof below establishes the stronger statement
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Consequently the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\). This is a strengthening of the requested infimum statement, not a relaxation.

## Proof

We prove first that every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), and then construct a realization for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

---

# Part I. Lower bound: a realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. Let \(\mathcal T_0\) be a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and let \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) be a realizing map.

## 1. Cevian lengthening and refinement

A clean triangle has exactly two vertices on one boundary line and one vertex on the other. The edge joining the two vertices on the same boundary line is its boundary edge; the opposite vertex is its apex.

### Lemma 1.1 — Cevian lengthening

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the apex. Let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be an affine squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is concave, so
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Therefore \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened. On the boundary edge, affineness gives a constant contraction factor strictly smaller than \(1\), so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened. \(\square\)

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) as follows. For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of \(\mathcal T_0\) lying on that boundary edge, and cone the resulting subintervals to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies that only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge, so each original triangle is subdivided into finitely many clean triangles.

The refinement is still countable, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant. By Lemma 1.1, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle: new boundary edges are shortened, and new cevians are lengthened.

After this refinement, the triangulation is face-to-face. Boundary vertex-in-edge incidences have been removed by construction. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, because two convex triangles whose edges cross transversely would have overlapping interiors near the crossing. Two collinear nonboundary edges joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) have the same endpoints and hence are the same edge. We write \(\mathcal T\) for the refined triangulation.

## 2. Descent to a finite embedded quotient cellulation

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) is a Euclidean isometry, the Euclidean metric on \(\Sigma\) descends to a flat metric on \(M_\beta\). The quotient is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is a single circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization hypothesis \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need finiteness of the quotient cellulation. Every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\): indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus if \(k\ne0\), then \(|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on a refined triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), and there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle.

Consequently no closed refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. For if
\[
z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta,\qquad k\ne0,
\]
then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, so there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of refined triangles. Therefore \(\mathcal T\) descends to a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\), and \(F\) is PL and embedded with respect to this cellulation.

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\). Indeed, subdivide the arc at the finitely many boundary vertices it meets; each nontrivial piece lies in a boundary edge of a clean triangle and is strictly shortened.

## 3. The leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider all cevians from the apex to points of the boundary edge. For each quotient triangle \(\tau\), these cevians are parametrized by a closed interval \(I_\tau\). The endpoints of \(I_\tau\) correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\tau\). Whenever two triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the corresponding endpoints of their intervals. The resulting finite graph is the quotient leaf graph, denoted \(\Lambda_M\).

Every nonboundary edge lies in the interior of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangles, one on each side. Hence every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals belong to \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, except for the two extreme boundary edges. Thus all triangles incident to a boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, and so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct similarly the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) using the refined triangulation of \(\Sigma\). The same open-closed argument in the connected strip \(\Sigma\) shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The quotient map \(\Sigma\to M_\beta\) induces a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has one endpoint \((x_0,0)\) and one endpoint \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it can fix the leaf only if \(n=0\). An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) would have to interchange the two endpoints, forcing
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible because \(\beta>0\). Therefore the covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, it is a line:
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from its endpoint on \(y=0\) to its endpoint on \(y=1\). By Lemma 1.1, the image of every lifted leaf is a nondegenerate segment, so it has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented support lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment of \(G_\beta\ell\) is the same geometric segment as the image of \(\ell\), but with opposite orientation. Thus along one fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), the path of oriented support lines starts at some oriented line and ends at the opposite oriented line.

## 4. An oriented-line lemma

### Lemma 1.2 — Perpendicular intersecting support lines

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct parameters determine unoriented support lines that are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by its unit direction \(u\in S^2\) and its moment
\[
m=p\times u,
\]
where \(p\) is any point on the line. The moment is independent of the chosen point \(p\), and reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all real \(s\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]

For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
and
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, for the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one checks that
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
Therefore the map \(s\mapsto\phi(s,1/2)\) has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the cylinder \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), so it must have degree \(0\), a contradiction.

Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by points \(p,q\) and directions \(u,v\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 1.2 to the oriented support-line path from the previous section gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect. Let those support lines be \(\ell_T\) and \(\ell_B\), and let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap\ell_B.
\]

## 5. The metric estimate

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the image segments of \(T\) and \(B\) cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel the two leaves if necessary so that the image segment \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) lifts to a segment joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\). By Lemma 1.1,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Thus
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because endpoints of \(T\) map to points on \(\ell_T\), while \(Q\) has positive distance from \(\ell_T\).

Let the endpoints of \(T\) be \(a,c\in\partial M_\beta\), and write
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The two boundary arcs between \(a\) and \(c\) have flat lengths summing to \(2\beta\). Let \(D\) be the one containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t
\]
for some real \(t\) with \(|t|<\beta\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+h,1).
\]
Parametrize the quotient boundary circle of length \(2\beta\) as follows: a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\pmod{2\beta}\), and a top point \((u,1)\) has coordinate \(u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}\). This is compatible with \(G_\beta\), since \(G_\beta(u,0)=(u+\beta,1)\), whose coordinate is \(u\).

Thus the boundary-coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), this implies
\[
h\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of this congruence class has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Hence
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
Therefore, by Lemma 1.1 applied to \(T\),
\[
L=|F(T)|>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. Since \(H\) is a boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L,
\]
where \(\ell_F(H)\) denotes the Euclidean length of the image path \(F(H)\). Boundary arcs are strictly shortened, so
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge L.
\]
Combining with (1),
\[
\beta-t>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, \(D\) is a boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) passing through \(q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary estimate
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}
\]
holds: the minimum occurs when the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) to the line \(AC\) is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|,
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

From (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

# Part II. Upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) and a realizing map.

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\),
\[
L<W.
\]
Choose a number \(\sigma\) with
\[
L<\sigma<W,
\]
and set
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L,\;W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

## 1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation

For \(j\in\mathbb Z\), define periodic boundary increments
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), and define \(c_j\) by
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j.
\]
Every three consecutive increments sum to \(W\), so every nine consecutive increments sum to \(3W=2\beta\). Hence
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left side of \(Q_j\) has \(x\)-coordinate
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus, for each fixed \(s\), the intervals
\[
[x_j(s),x_{j+1}(s)]
\]
tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{s\}\). Therefore the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\).

Split \(Q_j\) by the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), obtaining clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),
\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]
These triangles form a countable, locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

The action of \(G_\beta\) satisfies
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Hence
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) be the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since
\[
G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9},
\]
the indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Therefore \(D_j\) descends to the quotient triangle
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
and \(E_j\) descends to \(T_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has nine vertices \(v_0,\dots,v_8\) and nine faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

## 2. The target vertices

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in a horizontal plane in \(\mathbb R^3\), let \(o\) be its centroid, and let \(e_3\) be a unit vector perpendicular to that plane. For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3,\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\), both sufficiently small, and set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
For \(\eta,h\) small enough, every distance between points in different clusters
\[
\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\quad\text{and}\quad \{A_j,M_j,C_j\},\qquad i\ne j,
\]
lies in
\[
(\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4),
\]
while every distance between two points in the same cluster is \(<\lambda/2\).

Define nine target vertices:
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
Extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\). Define a quotient PL map \(\Phi\) by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j,
\]
affine on every face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

The nine target faces are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(T_0)&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\Phi(T_1)&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],\\
\Phi(T_2)&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],&
\Phi(T_3)&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],\\
\Phi(T_4)&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\Phi(T_5)&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\Phi(T_6)&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\Phi(T_7)&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],\\
\Phi(T_8)&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

## 3. Embeddedness of the target surface

The three horizontal triangular faces are
\[
[A_0,A_1,A_2],\qquad [M_1,M_2,M_0],\qquad [C_2,C_0,C_1].
\]
The remaining six faces form three side panels:
\[
R_{01}=\Phi(T_0)\cup\Phi(T_4),
\]
\[
R_{12}=\Phi(T_3)\cup\Phi(T_7),
\]
\[
R_{20}=\Phi(T_1)\cup\Phi(T_6).
\]
These panels lie over the sides \(01\), \(12\), and \(20\) of the original equilateral triangle.

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(p_0,p_1,p_2\). For a homothetic triangle of scale \(r\),
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\},
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\}.
\]
The side \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) opposite \(k\), where \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), is given by
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\}\cap\mathcal H(r,z).
\]
Thus adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only in their common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap \mathcal H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The horizontal slices of the side panels are:
\[
R_{01}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h},z\right),
\qquad h\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
R_{12}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h},z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
and
\[
R_{20}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh,z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le h.
\]

Now check slices.

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{12}\) and \(R_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
\[
1+\eta\frac z{2h}>1,
\qquad
1-\eta\frac zh<1,
\]
so the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) occur. Their scale difference is
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0.
\]
Hence those adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the horizontal triangle \([A_0,A_1,A_2]\) meets \(R_{12}\) along \([A_1,A_2]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([A_2,A_0]\), exactly as prescribed.

At \(z=h\), the middle triangle \([M_1,M_2,M_0]\) meets \(R_{01}\) along \([M_0,M_1]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([M_2,M_0]\). The section of \(R_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), while the middle triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), so that section lies outside the middle triangle and is disjoint from it.

At \(z=2h\), the top triangle \([C_2,C_0,C_1]\) meets \(R_{01}\) along \([C_0,C_1]\) and meets \(R_{12}\) along \([C_1,C_2]\), exactly as prescribed.

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so its two triangular faces meet only along that diagonal. Therefore the nine target triangles intersect only in their prescribed common edges and vertices. Hence \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

## 4. Squeeze inequalities

We verify that every face map is a squeeze map.

First consider boundary edges. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=\lambda,
\]
and the target edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) joins two points in the same cluster, so its length is \(<\lambda/2\).

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=W-2\lambda.
\]
The target edge joins two different clusters, so its length is \(<\sigma+\Delta/4\). Since
\[
\sigma\le W-\Delta,
\]
we have
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider a nonboundary quotient edge
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
It is lifted by the segment \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}.
\]
A direct computation from the periodic increments gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]

Every target nonboundary edge joins points in different clusters, so its target length is
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since
\[
\sigma\ge L+\Delta,
\]
we have
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore every affine face map is a squeeze map.

## 5. The lifted realizing map

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\).

On vertices,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Hence, by affineness on each triangle,
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]
So \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\). Since \(\Phi\) is injective,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p\text{ and }q\text{ have the same image in }\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining Part I and Part II,
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Consequently the infimum of the realized set is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]
\(\square\)

Consequently its infimum is sqrt(3). This strengthens the requested infimum statement and is not a relaxation.",

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

For every real number \(\beta>0\), let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), let \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\). For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the usual \(k\)-fold iterate, using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) when \(k<0\) and the identity when \(k=0\). A clean triangle means the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\); its unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) is called its boundary edge. A clean triangulation means a countable, locally finite collection of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\). It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle means an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the boundary edge and strictly lengthens the two other edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) which is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies
\[
f(p)=f(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z
\]
for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\).

The proof below establishes the stronger statement
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Consequently the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\). This is a strengthening of the requested infimum statement, not a relaxation.

## Proof

We prove first that every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), and then construct a realization for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

---

# Part I. Lower bound: a realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. Let \(\mathcal T_0\) be a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and let \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) be a realizing map.

## 1. Cevian lengthening and refinement

A clean triangle has exactly two vertices on one boundary line and one vertex on the other. The edge joining the two vertices on the same boundary line is its boundary edge; the opposite vertex is its apex.

### Lemma 1.1 — Cevian lengthening

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the apex. Let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be an affine squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is concave, so
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Therefore \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened. On the boundary edge, affineness gives a constant contraction factor strictly smaller than \(1\), so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened. \(\square\)

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) as follows. For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of \(\mathcal T_0\) lying on that boundary edge, and cone the resulting subintervals to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies that only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge, so each original triangle is subdivided into finitely many clean triangles.

The refinement is still countable, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant. By Lemma 1.1, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle: new boundary edges are shortened, and new cevians are lengthened.

After this refinement, the triangulation is face-to-face. Boundary vertex-in-edge incidences have been removed by construction. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, because two convex triangles whose edges cross transversely would have overlapping interiors near the crossing. Two collinear nonboundary edges joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) have the same endpoints and hence are the same edge. We write \(\mathcal T\) for the refined triangulation.

## 2. Descent to a finite embedded quotient cellulation

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) is a Euclidean isometry, the Euclidean metric on \(\Sigma\) descends to a flat metric on \(M_\beta\). The quotient is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is a single circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization hypothesis \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need finiteness of the quotient cellulation. Every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\): indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus if \(k\ne0\), then \(|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on a refined triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), and there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle.

Consequently no closed refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. For if
\[
z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta,\qquad k\ne0,
\]
then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, so there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of refined triangles. Therefore \(\mathcal T\) descends to a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\), and \(F\) is PL and embedded with respect to this cellulation.

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\). Indeed, subdivide the arc at the finitely many boundary vertices it meets; each nontrivial piece lies in a boundary edge of a clean triangle and is strictly shortened.

## 3. The leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider all cevians from the apex to points of the boundary edge. For each quotient triangle \(\tau\), these cevians are parametrized by a closed interval \(I_\tau\). The endpoints of \(I_\tau\) correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\tau\). Whenever two triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the corresponding endpoints of their intervals. The resulting finite graph is the quotient leaf graph, denoted \(\Lambda_M\).

Every nonboundary edge lies in the interior of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangles, one on each side. Hence every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals belong to \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, except for the two extreme boundary edges. Thus all triangles incident to a boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, and so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct similarly the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) using the refined triangulation of \(\Sigma\). The same open-closed argument in the connected strip \(\Sigma\) shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The quotient map \(\Sigma\to M_\beta\) induces a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has one endpoint \((x_0,0)\) and one endpoint \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it can fix the leaf only if \(n=0\). An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) would have to interchange the two endpoints, forcing
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible because \(\beta>0\). Therefore the covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, it is a line:
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from its endpoint on \(y=0\) to its endpoint on \(y=1\). By Lemma 1.1, the image of every lifted leaf is a nondegenerate segment, so it has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented support lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment of \(G_\beta\ell\) is the same geometric segment as the image of \(\ell\), but with opposite orientation. Thus along one fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), the path of oriented support lines starts at some oriented line and ends at the opposite oriented line.

## 4. An oriented-line lemma

### Lemma 1.2 — Perpendicular intersecting support lines

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct parameters determine unoriented support lines that are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by its unit direction \(u\in S^2\) and its moment
\[
m=p\times u,
\]
where \(p\) is any point on the line. The moment is independent of the chosen point \(p\), and reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all real \(s\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]

For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
and
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, for the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one checks that
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
Therefore the map \(s\mapsto\phi(s,1/2)\) has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the cylinder \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), so it must have degree \(0\), a contradiction.

Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by points \(p,q\) and directions \(u,v\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 1.2 to the oriented support-line path from the previous section gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect. Let those support lines be \(\ell_T\) and \(\ell_B\), and let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap\ell_B.
\]

## 5. The metric estimate

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the image segments of \(T\) and \(B\) cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel the two leaves if necessary so that the image segment \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) lifts to a segment joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\). By Lemma 1.1,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Thus
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because endpoints of \(T\) map to points on \(\ell_T\), while \(Q\) has positive distance from \(\ell_T\).

Let the endpoints of \(T\) be \(a,c\in\partial M_\beta\), and write
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The two boundary arcs between \(a\) and \(c\) have flat lengths summing to \(2\beta\). Let \(D\) be the one containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t
\]
for some real \(t\) with \(|t|<\beta\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+h,1).
\]
Parametrize the quotient boundary circle of length \(2\beta\) as follows: a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\pmod{2\beta}\), and a top point \((u,1)\) has coordinate \(u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}\). This is compatible with \(G_\beta\), since \(G_\beta(u,0)=(u+\beta,1)\), whose coordinate is \(u\).

Thus the boundary-coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), this implies
\[
h\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of this congruence class has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Hence
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
Therefore, by Lemma 1.1 applied to \(T\),
\[
L=|F(T)|>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. Since \(H\) is a boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L,
\]
where \(\ell_F(H)\) denotes the Euclidean length of the image path \(F(H)\). Boundary arcs are strictly shortened, so
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge L.
\]
Combining with (1),
\[
\beta-t>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, \(D\) is a boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) passing through \(q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary estimate
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}
\]
holds: the minimum occurs when the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) to the line \(AC\) is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|,
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

From (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

# Part II. Upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) and a realizing map.

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\),
\[
L<W.
\]
Choose a number \(\sigma\) with
\[
L<\sigma<W,
\]
and set
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L,\;W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

## 1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation

For \(j\in\mathbb Z\), define periodic boundary increments
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), and define \(c_j\) by
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j.
\]
Every three consecutive increments sum to \(W\), so every nine consecutive increments sum to \(3W=2\beta\). Hence
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left side of \(Q_j\) has \(x\)-coordinate
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus, for each fixed \(s\), the intervals
\[
[x_j(s),x_{j+1}(s)]
\]
tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{s\}\). Therefore the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\).

Split \(Q_j\) by the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), obtaining clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),
\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]
These triangles form a countable, locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

The action of \(G_\beta\) satisfies
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Hence
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) be the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since
\[
G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9},
\]
the indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Therefore \(D_j\) descends to the quotient triangle
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
and \(E_j\) descends to \(T_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has nine vertices \(v_0,\dots,v_8\) and nine faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

## 2. The target vertices

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in a horizontal plane in \(\mathbb R^3\), let \(o\) be its centroid, and let \(e_3\) be a unit vector perpendicular to that plane. For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3,\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\), both sufficiently small, and set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
For \(\eta,h\) small enough, every distance between points in different clusters
\[
\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\quad\text{and}\quad \{A_j,M_j,C_j\},\qquad i\ne j,
\]
lies in
\[
(\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4),
\]
while every distance between two points in the same cluster is \(<\lambda/2\).

Define nine target vertices:
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
Extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\). Define a quotient PL map \(\Phi\) by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j,
\]
affine on every face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

The nine target faces are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(T_0)&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\Phi(T_1)&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],\\
\Phi(T_2)&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],&
\Phi(T_3)&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],\\
\Phi(T_4)&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\Phi(T_5)&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\Phi(T_6)&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\Phi(T_7)&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],\\
\Phi(T_8)&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

## 3. Embeddedness of the target surface

The three horizontal triangular faces are
\[
[A_0,A_1,A_2],\qquad [M_1,M_2,M_0],\qquad [C_2,C_0,C_1].
\]
The remaining six faces form three side panels:
\[
R_{01}=\Phi(T_0)\cup\Phi(T_4),
\]
\[
R_{12}=\Phi(T_3)\cup\Phi(T_7),
\]
\[
R_{20}=\Phi(T_1)\cup\Phi(T_6).
\]
These panels lie over the sides \(01\), \(12\), and \(20\) of the original equilateral triangle.

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(p_0,p_1,p_2\). For a homothetic triangle of scale \(r\),
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\},
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\}.
\]
The side \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) opposite \(k\), where \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), is given by
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\}\cap\mathcal H(r,z).
\]
Thus adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only in their common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap \mathcal H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The horizontal slices of the side panels are:
\[
R_{01}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h},z\right),
\qquad h\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
R_{12}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h},z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
and
\[
R_{20}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh,z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le h.
\]

Now check slices.

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{12}\) and \(R_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
\[
1+\eta\frac z{2h}>1,
\qquad
1-\eta\frac zh<1,
\]
so the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) occur. Their scale difference is
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0.
\]
Hence those adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the horizontal triangle \([A_0,A_1,A_2]\) meets \(R_{12}\) along \([A_1,A_2]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([A_2,A_0]\), exactly as prescribed.

At \(z=h\), the middle triangle \([M_1,M_2,M_0]\) meets \(R_{01}\) along \([M_0,M_1]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([M_2,M_0]\). The section of \(R_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), while the middle triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), so that section lies outside the middle triangle and is disjoint from it.

At \(z=2h\), the top triangle \([C_2,C_0,C_1]\) meets \(R_{01}\) along \([C_0,C_1]\) and meets \(R_{12}\) along \([C_1,C_2]\), exactly as prescribed.

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so its two triangular faces meet only along that diagonal. Therefore the nine target triangles intersect only in their prescribed common edges and vertices. Hence \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

## 4. Squeeze inequalities

We verify that every face map is a squeeze map.

First consider boundary edges. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=\lambda,
\]
and the target edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) joins two points in the same cluster, so its length is \(<\lambda/2\).

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=W-2\lambda.
\]
The target edge joins two different clusters, so its length is \(<\sigma+\Delta/4\). Since
\[
\sigma\le W-\Delta,
\]
we have
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider a nonboundary quotient edge
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
It is lifted by the segment \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}.
\]
A direct computation from the periodic increments gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]

Every target nonboundary edge joins points in different clusters, so its target length is
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since
\[
\sigma\ge L+\Delta,
\]
we have
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore every affine face map is a squeeze map.

## 5. The lifted realizing map

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\).

On vertices,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Hence, by affineness on each triangle,
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]
So \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\). Since \(\Phi\) is injective,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p\text{ and }q\text{ have the same image in }\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining Part I and Part II,
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Consequently the infimum of the realized set is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]
\(\square\)
self_declared_problem_solved: For every real number beta > 0, with Sigma = R x [0,1], boundary partial Sigma = R x {0,1}, G_beta(x,y) = (x+beta,1-y), and with clean triangles, clean triangulations, squeeze maps, and realized numbers defined exactly as in the original problem, the set of realized numbers is exactly (sqrt(3), infinity). Consequently its infimum is sqrt(3). This strengthens the requested infimum statement and is not a relaxation.

Consequently its infimum is sqrt(3). This strengthens the requested infimum statement and is not a relaxation.

description: writeup (full problem): The realized numbers β > 0 are exactly the interval (√3,∞), and therefore the infimum of the set of realized numbers is √3.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

For every real number \(\beta>0\), let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), let \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\). For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the \(k\)-fold iterate, with negative \(k\) meaning iterates of \(G_\beta^{-1}\). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). A clean triangulation is a countable locally finite covering of \(\Sigma\) by clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors. It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) preserves the collection of triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) which is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies \(f(p)=f(q)\) exactly when \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).  

The proof below establishes the stronger theorem:
\[
\beta \text{ is realized } \Longleftrightarrow \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), so its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This strengthens the requested conclusion and is not a relaxation.

## Proof

We prove the two implications separately.

---

# I. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Assume \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Put
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},
\qquad
L_0=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Then
\[
W>L_0
\]
because \(W^2>L_0^2\) is equivalent to \(4\beta^2/9>1+\beta^2/9\), i.e. \(\beta^2>3\).

Choose
\[
L_0<\sigma<W,
\]
and set
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_0,\;W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

## 1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation of the strip

Define a positive sequence \(\ell_j\), \(j\in\mathbb Z\), by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus every block of three consecutive lengths has sum
\[
\lambda+\lambda+(W-2\lambda)=W.
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), and define \(c_j\) by
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+3}=c_j+W,
\qquad
c_{j+9}=c_j+3W=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),
\qquad
Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)
=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Therefore, for each fixed \(s\), the intervals
\[
[x_j(s),x_{j+1}(s)]
\]
tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{s\}\). Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\) as \(j\to\pm\infty\), the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\). This gives two clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),
\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]

The action of \(G_\beta\) is compatible with this tiling. Indeed,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Hence
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
Thus the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) denote the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since
\[
G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9},
\]
the index \(j\) is taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\), because \(Y_j=G_\beta(X_{j-4})\). Therefore \(D_j\) descends to the quotient triangle
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
with all indices modulo \(9\). The triangle \(E_j\) descends to \(T_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has exactly the nine faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

## 2. The target \(9\)-triangle Möbius band

Let \(a_0,a_1,a_2\in\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\subset\mathbb R^3\) be the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\), centered at the origin. Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), write
\[
q_i(r,z)=r a_i+z e_3.
\]

Choose small numbers \(\eta>0\) and \(h>0\). Define three horizontal homothetic copies:

\[
H_0:\quad q_i(1,0),
\]
\[
H_1:\quad q_i(1-\eta,h),
\]
\[
H_2:\quad q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]

Assign target vertices \(P_j\in\mathbb R^3\) as follows:
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0(1+\eta,2h)&
q_0(1-\eta,h)&
q_0(1,0)&
q_1(1,0)&
q_1(1+\eta,2h)&
q_1(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1,0)&
q_2(1+\eta,2h).
\end{array}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
H_0=(v_2,v_3,v_7),\qquad
H_1=(v_1,v_5,v_6),\qquad
H_2=(v_0,v_4,v_8).
\]

As \(\eta,h\to0\), vertices in the same cluster near a fixed \(a_i\) approach one another, while vertices in different clusters have distances approaching \(\sigma\). Hence we may choose \(\eta,h>0\) so small that:

1. every same-cluster target edge has length \(<\lambda/2\);
2. every different-cluster target edge has length in
   \[
   (\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4).
   \]

Define a PL map \(F\) on the quotient by
\[
F(v_j)=P_j,
\]
and extend affinely over every face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

The nine target faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
F(T_0)&=[P_0,P_1,P_5],&
F(T_1)&=[P_1,P_2,P_6],&
F(T_2)&=[P_2,P_3,P_7],\\
F(T_3)&=[P_3,P_4,P_8],&
F(T_4)&=[P_4,P_5,P_0],&
F(T_5)&=[P_5,P_6,P_1],\\
F(T_6)&=[P_6,P_7,P_2],&
F(T_7)&=[P_7,P_8,P_3],&
F(T_8)&=[P_8,P_0,P_4].
\end{aligned}
\]
The faces \(T_2,T_5,T_8\) are the three horizontal triangles \(H_0,H_1,H_2\). The remaining six faces form three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
R_{01}=F(T_0)\cup F(T_4),
\]
joining the side between \(a_0\) and \(a_1\);
\[
R_{12}=F(T_3)\cup F(T_7),
\]
joining the side between \(a_1\) and \(a_2\);
\[
R_{20}=F(T_1)\cup F(T_6),
\]
joining the side between \(a_2\) and \(a_0\).

## 3. Embeddedness of the target surface

We prove that these nine triangles meet only along their prescribed common edges and vertices.

For \(r>0\), let
\[
\Delta(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{r a_0,r a_1,r a_2\}
\subset \mathbb R^2.
\]
Let \(S_{ij}(r)\) be the side segment of \(\Delta(r)\) joining \(r a_i\) to \(r a_j\).

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(a_0,a_1,a_2\). Since the centroid is \(0\), one has
\[
\Delta(r)=\left\{\lambda_i\ge\frac{1-r}{3}\text{ for }i=0,1,2\right\}.
\]
The side \(S_{ij}(r)\), opposite the remaining index \(k\), is given by
\[
S_{ij}(r)=
\left\{
\lambda_k=\frac{1-r}{3},\;
\lambda_i,\lambda_j\ge \frac{1-r}{3}
\right\}.
\]

It follows immediately that two adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and if \(r=s\), they meet only at their common endpoint. Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r)\cap \Delta(s)=\varnothing.
\]

Now examine horizontal slices. The panel \(R_{20}\) occurs for \(0\le z\le h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh\right).
\]
The panel \(R_{12}\) occurs for \(0\le z\le2h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right).
\]
The panel \(R_{01}\) occurs for \(h\le z\le2h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right).
\]

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{20}\) and \(R_{12}\) appear. Their scales are respectively
\[
1-\eta z/h<1
\]
and
\[
1+\eta z/(2h)>1,
\]
so the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) appear. Their scales satisfy
\[
1+\eta\frac z{2h}
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0,
\]
so again the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the bottom horizontal triangle \(H_0=\Delta(1)\) meets the panels exactly along the sides \(S_{20}(1)\) and \(S_{12}(1)\). At \(z=h\), the middle triangle \(H_1=\Delta(1-\eta)\) meets the panels \(R_{20}\) and \(R_{01}\) exactly along two of its sides, while the slice of \(R_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2>1-\eta\) and is disjoint from \(\Delta(1-\eta)\). At \(z=2h\), the top triangle \(H_2=\Delta(1+\eta)\) meets the panels exactly along the sides \(S_{01}(1+\eta)\) and \(S_{12}(1+\eta)\).

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles in a panel meet exactly along that diagonal. The slice analysis shows that different panels and horizontal triangles have no additional intersections. Therefore \(F\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

## 4. Squeeze inequalities

We now compare flat edge lengths in the domain with target edge lengths.

### Boundary edges

The quotient boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) are in the same cluster. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda=\ell_j.
\]

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) are in different clusters. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}.
\]
Because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\),
\[
W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### Nonboundary edges

Consider the nonboundary edge represented by \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|^2=1+(d_j-\beta)^2.
\]
A direct computation from the length pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\), we get
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\beta/3
\]
using \(\lambda<\beta/12\). Hence
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_0.
\]

The other type of nonboundary edge is \(X_{j+1}Y_j\). Its horizontal displacement is
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}
=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it satisfies the same estimate. Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length \(<L_0\).

On the target side, every nonboundary edge joins vertices from different clusters, and hence has length
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_0\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4\ge L_0+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L_0.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(F\), and hence its lift \(f\), is a squeeze map on each clean triangle.

## 5. The lifted map and the orbit condition

Define \(P_{j+9}=P_j\). On the strip, set
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,
\qquad
f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over each \(D_j\) and \(E_j\). The vertex assignments agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous.

Also,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
By affineness on corresponding triangles,
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]
Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\). Therefore
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p,q\) project to the same point in the quotient, which is equivalent to
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Hence every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

# II. The lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume now that \(\beta>0\) is realized. Let \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) be a realizing map for a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

## 1. Cevian lengthening and face-to-face refinement

A clean triangle has one edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it the boundary edge. The opposite vertex is its apex.

### Lemma 1: Cevian lengthening

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\), and let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

Proof. Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Because \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\Bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\Bigr)<0,
\]
since the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave. In particular,
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Thus \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened.

On the boundary edge, \(A\) is affine with contraction factor
\[
\frac{|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|}{|r_1-r_0|}<1,
\]
so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened. \(\square\)

Now refine the triangulation as follows. In each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of the original triangulation lying on that boundary edge, and cone those subdivision points to the apex. Local finiteness guarantees that only finitely many such subdivision points occur on each compact boundary edge. Performing this construction equivariantly gives a new countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

By Lemma 1, \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every new triangle.

After this refinement, the triangulation is face-to-face. Boundary vertex-in-edge incidences have been removed by construction. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: if two such edges crossed, the interiors of the adjacent triangles would occupy two open half-planes with nonempty overlap near the crossing point. Finally, two collinear nonboundary edges must coincide, because a line crossing the strip from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) has a unique endpoint on each boundary component. Thus cell intersections are common faces.

From now on, use this refined face-to-face triangulation.

## 2. The embedded PL Möbius band quotient

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence \([p]=[q]\). Therefore \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is an embedding.

The quotient cellulation is finite. Indeed, a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles by local finiteness, and every triangle has some translate meeting that rectangle.

We also need that the quotient has no self-identified triangle. Every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\). For even powers,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and for odd powers,
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus if \(k\ne0\), then
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta.
\]

If \(f\) were not injective on some refined triangle, then because \(f\) is affine there would be distinct points \(p,q\) in that triangle with \(|p-q|<\beta\) and \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting the distance bound. Hence \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle.

Consequently a closed refined triangle cannot meet a nontrivial translate of itself. Thus the quotient is a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\).

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): subdivide the arc at boundary vertices and at its endpoints; on each nontrivial subarc inside a boundary edge, the affine restriction has contraction factor \(<1\).

## 3. The leaf graph

In each clean triangle, consider all cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. These cevians are naturally parametrized by an interval. Glue endpoints of these intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge of the quotient triangulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), called the leaf graph.

Each vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) corresponds to a nonboundary edge. Since the triangulation is face-to-face and \(M_\beta\) is a surface, every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\).

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals belong to \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes. Hence all triangles incident to a boundary vertex also belong to the same \(U_C\).

It follows that \(U_C\) is open in \(M_\beta\). Since the quotient cellulation is finite, \(U_C\) is also closed. The Möbius band is connected, so there is only one component. Thus
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) in the strip. The same open-closed argument applies in \(\Sigma\); local finiteness ensures that a union of closed triangles from a subcollection is closed. Hence \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
has infinite degree. Indeed, no nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. If a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\qquad
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, every leaf is strictly lengthened, so its image is a nondegenerate segment and has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). Along one fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), the endpoint leaf is obtained from the initial leaf by applying \(G_\beta\). Since \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the oriented image line is reversed. Thus we get a continuous path of oriented affine lines whose endpoint is the same underlying line with opposite orientation.

## 4. An oriented-line lemma

### Lemma 2

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist two distinct parameters \(s\ne t\) such that the underlying unoriented lines of \(L_s\) and \(L_t\) are perpendicular and intersect.

Proof. Represent an oriented affine line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all real \(s\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),
\qquad
(a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map from the cylinder \(S^1\times[0,1]\) to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. But on \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A map \(S^1\to S^1\) with this antipodal symmetry has odd degree, whereas it is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), which has degree \(0\). This is impossible. Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere, with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two direction vectors are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel; the equation \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 2 to the oriented support-line path from the leaf graph gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect. Let those support lines be \(\ell_T\) and \(\ell_B\), and let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap \ell_B.
\]

## 5. The metric estimate

The relative interiors of distinct leaves are disjoint in \(M_\beta\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel the two leaves if necessary so that the image segment \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The flat length of any lifted leaf is at least \(1\), because it joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, \(B\) is strictly lengthened, so
\[
|F(B)|>1.
\]
Thus
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because endpoints of \(T\) map into \(\ell_T\).

Let \(A\) and \(C\) be the endpoints of the segment \(F(T)\), and put
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
Let the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\) have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\beta+t,
\]
with \(q\) lying on the latter arc. Call the arc not containing \(q\) by \(H\), and the arc containing \(q\) by \(D\). Thus
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\qquad
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),
\qquad
(x+h,1).
\]
Parametrize the quotient boundary circle of length \(2\beta\) by the coordinate
\[
\rho(u,0)=u\pmod{2\beta},
\qquad
\rho(u,1)=u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
This is well-defined under \(G_\beta\). The coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is therefore
\[
h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\), this implies
\[
h\equiv \pm t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), the representative \(h\) satisfies
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
The flat length of \(T\) is \(\sqrt{1+h^2}\), and \(T\) is strictly lengthened, so
\[
L>\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. The arc \(H\) connects the endpoints of \(T\), so its image path connects \(A\) to \(C\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
But \(H\) is strictly shortened:
\[
\ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Hence, using (1),
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, the arc \(D\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary broken-path estimate gives
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting \(Q\) to the line \(AC\), the sum is minimized when the projection is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Since \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]

Finally, for every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
To see this, suppose both terms were \(<\sqrt3\). Then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
implies, after squaring,
\[
t<\frac1{\sqrt3}.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
implies, after squaring,
\[
t>\frac1{\sqrt3}.
\]
This is impossible. Equality occurs only at \(t=1/\sqrt3\).

Thus every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Together with the explicit construction for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have proved
\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Therefore the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]
\(\square\)

The clean triangulation is\n$G_{\\beta}$-{\\it invariant\\/} if the action of $G_{\\beta}$ preserves\nthe triangulation.\n\nA {\\it squeeze map\\/} of a clean triangle $\\tau$\nis an affine map from $\\tau$ into $\\R^3$ which decreases the\nlength of the edge of $\\tau$ that is contained in\n$\\partial \\Sigma$ and increases the\nlengths of the other two edges of $\\tau$.\n\nCall the number $\\beta>0$ {\\it realized\\/} if there is a\ncontinuous map $f: \\Sigma \\to \\R^3$\nwith the following properties.\n\\begin{enumerate}\n\\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a\n  $G_{\\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\\Sigma$.\n  \\item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k\n    \\in \\Z$.\n    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \\in \\Sigma$.\n  \\end{enumerate}\n  Prove that $\\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.",

Consequently the set of realized numbers is (sqrt(3), infinity), so its infimum is sqrt(3).",
