solution:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

For every real number \(\beta>0\), let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), let \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\). For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the \(k\)-fold iterate, with negative \(k\) meaning iterates of \(G_\beta^{-1}\). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). A clean triangulation is a countable locally finite covering of \(\Sigma\) by clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors. It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) preserves the collection of triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) which is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies \(f(p)=f(q)\) exactly when \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).  

The proof below establishes the stronger theorem:
\[
\beta \text{ is realized } \Longleftrightarrow \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), so its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This strengthens the requested conclusion and is not a relaxation.

## Proof

We prove the two implications separately.

---

# I. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Assume \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Put
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},
\qquad
L_0=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Then
\[
W>L_0
\]
because \(W^2>L_0^2\) is equivalent to \(4\beta^2/9>1+\beta^2/9\), i.e. \(\beta^2>3\).

Choose
\[
L_0<\sigma<W,
\]
and set
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_0,\;W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

## 1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation of the strip

Define a positive sequence \(\ell_j\), \(j\in\mathbb Z\), by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus every block of three consecutive lengths has sum
\[
\lambda+\lambda+(W-2\lambda)=W.
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), and define \(c_j\) by
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+3}=c_j+W,
\qquad
c_{j+9}=c_j+3W=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),
\qquad
Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)
=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Therefore, for each fixed \(s\), the intervals
\[
[x_j(s),x_{j+1}(s)]
\]
tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{s\}\). Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\) as \(j\to\pm\infty\), the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\). This gives two clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),
\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]

The action of \(G_\beta\) is compatible with this tiling. Indeed,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Hence
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
Thus the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) denote the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since
\[
G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9},
\]
the index \(j\) is taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\), because \(Y_j=G_\beta(X_{j-4})\). Therefore \(D_j\) descends to the quotient triangle
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
with all indices modulo \(9\). The triangle \(E_j\) descends to \(T_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has exactly the nine faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

## 2. The target \(9\)-triangle Möbius band

Let \(a_0,a_1,a_2\in\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\subset\mathbb R^3\) be the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\), centered at the origin. Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), write
\[
q_i(r,z)=r a_i+z e_3.
\]

Choose small numbers \(\eta>0\) and \(h>0\). Define three horizontal homothetic copies:

\[
H_0:\quad q_i(1,0),
\]
\[
H_1:\quad q_i(1-\eta,h),
\]
\[
H_2:\quad q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]

Assign target vertices \(P_j\in\mathbb R^3\) as follows:
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0(1+\eta,2h)&
q_0(1-\eta,h)&
q_0(1,0)&
q_1(1,0)&
q_1(1+\eta,2h)&
q_1(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1,0)&
q_2(1+\eta,2h).
\end{array}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
H_0=(v_2,v_3,v_7),\qquad
H_1=(v_1,v_5,v_6),\qquad
H_2=(v_0,v_4,v_8).
\]

As \(\eta,h\to0\), vertices in the same cluster near a fixed \(a_i\) approach one another, while vertices in different clusters have distances approaching \(\sigma\). Hence we may choose \(\eta,h>0\) so small that:

1. every same-cluster target edge has length \(<\lambda/2\);
2. every different-cluster target edge has length in
   \[
   (\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4).
   \]

Define a PL map \(F\) on the quotient by
\[
F(v_j)=P_j,
\]
and extend affinely over every face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

The nine target faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
F(T_0)&=[P_0,P_1,P_5],&
F(T_1)&=[P_1,P_2,P_6],&
F(T_2)&=[P_2,P_3,P_7],\\
F(T_3)&=[P_3,P_4,P_8],&
F(T_4)&=[P_4,P_5,P_0],&
F(T_5)&=[P_5,P_6,P_1],\\
F(T_6)&=[P_6,P_7,P_2],&
F(T_7)&=[P_7,P_8,P_3],&
F(T_8)&=[P_8,P_0,P_4].
\end{aligned}
\]
The faces \(T_2,T_5,T_8\) are the three horizontal triangles \(H_0,H_1,H_2\). The remaining six faces form three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
R_{01}=F(T_0)\cup F(T_4),
\]
joining the side between \(a_0\) and \(a_1\);
\[
R_{12}=F(T_3)\cup F(T_7),
\]
joining the side between \(a_1\) and \(a_2\);
\[
R_{20}=F(T_1)\cup F(T_6),
\]
joining the side between \(a_2\) and \(a_0\).

## 3. Embeddedness of the target surface

We prove that these nine triangles meet only along their prescribed common edges and vertices.

For \(r>0\), let
\[
\Delta(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{r a_0,r a_1,r a_2\}
\subset \mathbb R^2.
\]
Let \(S_{ij}(r)\) be the side segment of \(\Delta(r)\) joining \(r a_i\) to \(r a_j\).

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(a_0,a_1,a_2\). Since the centroid is \(0\), one has
\[
\Delta(r)=\left\{\lambda_i\ge\frac{1-r}{3}\text{ for }i=0,1,2\right\}.
\]
The side \(S_{ij}(r)\), opposite the remaining index \(k\), is given by
\[
S_{ij}(r)=
\left\{
\lambda_k=\frac{1-r}{3},\;
\lambda_i,\lambda_j\ge \frac{1-r}{3}
\right\}.
\]

It follows immediately that two adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and if \(r=s\), they meet only at their common endpoint. Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r)\cap \Delta(s)=\varnothing.
\]

Now examine horizontal slices. The panel \(R_{20}\) occurs for \(0\le z\le h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh\right).
\]
The panel \(R_{12}\) occurs for \(0\le z\le2h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right).
\]
The panel \(R_{01}\) occurs for \(h\le z\le2h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right).
\]

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{20}\) and \(R_{12}\) appear. Their scales are respectively
\[
1-\eta z/h<1
\]
and
\[
1+\eta z/(2h)>1,
\]
so the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) appear. Their scales satisfy
\[
1+\eta\frac z{2h}
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0,
\]
so again the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the bottom horizontal triangle \(H_0=\Delta(1)\) meets the panels exactly along the sides \(S_{20}(1)\) and \(S_{12}(1)\). At \(z=h\), the middle triangle \(H_1=\Delta(1-\eta)\) meets the panels \(R_{20}\) and \(R_{01}\) exactly along two of its sides, while the slice of \(R_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2>1-\eta\) and is disjoint from \(\Delta(1-\eta)\). At \(z=2h\), the top triangle \(H_2=\Delta(1+\eta)\) meets the panels exactly along the sides \(S_{01}(1+\eta)\) and \(S_{12}(1+\eta)\).

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles in a panel meet exactly along that diagonal. The slice analysis shows that different panels and horizontal triangles have no additional intersections. Therefore \(F\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

## 4. Squeeze inequalities

We now compare flat edge lengths in the domain with target edge lengths.

### Boundary edges

The quotient boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) are in the same cluster. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda=\ell_j.
\]

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) are in different clusters. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}.
\]
Because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\),
\[
W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### Nonboundary edges

Consider the nonboundary edge represented by \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|^2=1+(d_j-\beta)^2.
\]
A direct computation from the length pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\), we get
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\beta/3
\]
using \(\lambda<\beta/12\). Hence
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_0.
\]

The other type of nonboundary edge is \(X_{j+1}Y_j\). Its horizontal displacement is
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}
=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it satisfies the same estimate. Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length \(<L_0\).

On the target side, every nonboundary edge joins vertices from different clusters, and hence has length
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_0\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4\ge L_0+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L_0.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(F\), and hence its lift \(f\), is a squeeze map on each clean triangle.

## 5. The lifted map and the orbit condition

Define \(P_{j+9}=P_j\). On the strip, set
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,
\qquad
f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over each \(D_j\) and \(E_j\). The vertex assignments agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous.

Also,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
By affineness on corresponding triangles,
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]
Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\). Therefore
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p,q\) project to the same point in the quotient, which is equivalent to
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Hence every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

# II. The lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume now that \(\beta>0\) is realized. Let \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) be a realizing map for a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

## 1. Cevian lengthening and face-to-face refinement

A clean triangle has one edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it the boundary edge. The opposite vertex is its apex.

### Lemma 1: Cevian lengthening

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\), and let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

Proof. Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Because \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\Bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\Bigr)<0,
\]
since the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave. In particular,
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Thus \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened.

On the boundary edge, \(A\) is affine with contraction factor
\[
\frac{|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|}{|r_1-r_0|}<1,
\]
so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened. \(\square\)

Now refine the triangulation as follows. In each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of the original triangulation lying on that boundary edge, and cone those subdivision points to the apex. Local finiteness guarantees that only finitely many such subdivision points occur on each compact boundary edge. Performing this construction equivariantly gives a new countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

By Lemma 1, \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every new triangle.

After this refinement, the triangulation is face-to-face. Boundary vertex-in-edge incidences have been removed by construction. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: if two such edges crossed, the interiors of the adjacent triangles would occupy two open half-planes with nonempty overlap near the crossing point. Finally, two collinear nonboundary edges must coincide, because a line crossing the strip from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) has a unique endpoint on each boundary component. Thus cell intersections are common faces.

From now on, use this refined face-to-face triangulation.

## 2. The embedded PL Möbius band quotient

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence \([p]=[q]\). Therefore \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is an embedding.

The quotient cellulation is finite. Indeed, a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles by local finiteness, and every triangle has some translate meeting that rectangle.

We also need that the quotient has no self-identified triangle. Every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\). For even powers,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and for odd powers,
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus if \(k\ne0\), then
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta.
\]

If \(f\) were not injective on some refined triangle, then because \(f\) is affine there would be distinct points \(p,q\) in that triangle with \(|p-q|<\beta\) and \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting the distance bound. Hence \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle.

Consequently a closed refined triangle cannot meet a nontrivial translate of itself. Thus the quotient is a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\).

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): subdivide the arc at boundary vertices and at its endpoints; on each nontrivial subarc inside a boundary edge, the affine restriction has contraction factor \(<1\).

## 3. The leaf graph

In each clean triangle, consider all cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. These cevians are naturally parametrized by an interval. Glue endpoints of these intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge of the quotient triangulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), called the leaf graph.

Each vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) corresponds to a nonboundary edge. Since the triangulation is face-to-face and \(M_\beta\) is a surface, every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\).

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals belong to \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes. Hence all triangles incident to a boundary vertex also belong to the same \(U_C\).

It follows that \(U_C\) is open in \(M_\beta\). Since the quotient cellulation is finite, \(U_C\) is also closed. The Möbius band is connected, so there is only one component. Thus
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) in the strip. The same open-closed argument applies in \(\Sigma\); local finiteness ensures that a union of closed triangles from a subcollection is closed. Hence \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
has infinite degree. Indeed, no nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. If a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\qquad
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, every leaf is strictly lengthened, so its image is a nondegenerate segment and has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). Along one fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), the endpoint leaf is obtained from the initial leaf by applying \(G_\beta\). Since \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the oriented image line is reversed. Thus we get a continuous path of oriented affine lines whose endpoint is the same underlying line with opposite orientation.

## 4. An oriented-line lemma

### Lemma 2

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist two distinct parameters \(s\ne t\) such that the underlying unoriented lines of \(L_s\) and \(L_t\) are perpendicular and intersect.

Proof. Represent an oriented affine line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all real \(s\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),
\qquad
(a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map from the cylinder \(S^1\times[0,1]\) to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. But on \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A map \(S^1\to S^1\) with this antipodal symmetry has odd degree, whereas it is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), which has degree \(0\). This is impossible. Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere, with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two direction vectors are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel; the equation \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 2 to the oriented support-line path from the leaf graph gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect. Let those support lines be \(\ell_T\) and \(\ell_B\), and let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap \ell_B.
\]

## 5. The metric estimate

The relative interiors of distinct leaves are disjoint in \(M_\beta\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel the two leaves if necessary so that the image segment \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The flat length of any lifted leaf is at least \(1\), because it joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, \(B\) is strictly lengthened, so
\[
|F(B)|>1.
\]
Thus
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because endpoints of \(T\) map into \(\ell_T\).

Let \(A\) and \(C\) be the endpoints of the segment \(F(T)\), and put
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
Let the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\) have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\beta+t,
\]
with \(q\) lying on the latter arc. Call the arc not containing \(q\) by \(H\), and the arc containing \(q\) by \(D\). Thus
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\qquad
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),
\qquad
(x+h,1).
\]
Parametrize the quotient boundary circle of length \(2\beta\) by the coordinate
\[
\rho(u,0)=u\pmod{2\beta},
\qquad
\rho(u,1)=u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
This is well-defined under \(G_\beta\). The coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is therefore
\[
h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\), this implies
\[
h\equiv \pm t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), the representative \(h\) satisfies
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
The flat length of \(T\) is \(\sqrt{1+h^2}\), and \(T\) is strictly lengthened, so
\[
L>\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. The arc \(H\) connects the endpoints of \(T\), so its image path connects \(A\) to \(C\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
But \(H\) is strictly shortened:
\[
\ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Hence, using (1),
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, the arc \(D\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary broken-path estimate gives
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting \(Q\) to the line \(AC\), the sum is minimized when the projection is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Since \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]

Finally, for every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
To see this, suppose both terms were \(<\sqrt3\). Then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
implies, after squaring,
\[
t<\frac1{\sqrt3}.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
implies, after squaring,
\[
t>\frac1{\sqrt3}.
\]
This is impossible. Equality occurs only at \(t=1/\sqrt3\).

Thus every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Together with the explicit construction for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have proved
\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Therefore the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]
\(\square\)

Consequently the set of realized numbers is (sqrt(3), infinity), so its infimum is sqrt(3).

description: writeup (full problem): The realized numbers β > 0 are exactly the interval (√3, ∞), and therefore the infimum of the set of realized numbers is √3.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers and \(\mathbb R\) the reals. For \(\beta>0\), let
\[
\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1],\qquad \partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\},
\]
and let
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).
\]
For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), \(G_\beta^k\) denotes the \(k\)-fold iterate of \(G_\beta\), using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) when \(k<0\), and \(G_\beta^0\) is the identity. A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). Its unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) is called its boundary edge. A clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) is a countable locally finite family of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\). It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the boundary edge and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there are a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) whose restriction to every triangle is a squeeze map and such that, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
The proof below establishes the stronger exact classification:
\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This is not a relaxation of the original problem; it proves the original infimum assertion and identifies the realized set exactly.

## Proof

### I. The lower bound: a realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. Write \(G=G_\beta\). Thus there is a \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) satisfying the two realization properties.

#### 1. Cevian lengthening and a face-to-face refinement

We first record a useful elementary consequence of the squeeze condition.

**Lemma 1.** Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge, and let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For every point \(r=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), \(0\le s\le1\), the cevian \([a,r]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r)|^2-|a-r|^2.
\]
Since the two nonboundary sides are lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave and lies strictly above its chord on \(0<s<1\). Hence \(q(s)>0\) for all \(s\in[0,1]\). The restriction of \(A\) to the boundary edge is an affine contraction by a factor \(<1\), so all nontrivial boundary subsegments are shortened. \(\square\)

We now refine \(\mathcal T_0\). For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every triangulation vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies that only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge, so this produces a countable locally finite refinement. It is still \(G\)-invariant. By Lemma 1, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

Denote the refined triangulation by \(\mathcal T\). It may be taken to be face-to-face: boundary vertex-in-edge incidences and boundary edge overlaps have been subdivided away; transverse crossings of nonboundary edges would force overlap of triangular interiors in a small sector; and two collinear nonboundary edges cannot overlap properly, because a line joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) has unique endpoints on those two boundary lines. Thus distinct closed triangles of \(\mathcal T\) meet, if at all, in a common face.

#### 2. Passage to an embedded finite PL Möbius band

The quotient
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle
\]
is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f(Gp)=f(p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3,\qquad F([p])=f(p).
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition \(p=G^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need a finite regular quotient triangulation. For \(k\ne0\),
\[
G^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),\qquad
G^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y),
\]
so every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\).

If \(f\) were noninjective on some refined triangle \(\Delta\), then the affine linear part of \(f|_\Delta\) would have rank \(<2\), giving arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every closed triangle.

Consequently no closed triangle \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\) meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in\Delta\cap G^k\Delta\), \(k\ne0\), then \(z=G^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\); injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), impossible because the deck action is free.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, and every triangle has some translate meeting it. Therefore \(\mathcal T/\langle G\rangle\) is a finite regular clean triangulation of \(M_\beta\), and \(F\) is affine on each quotient triangle.

Finally, \(F\) strictly shortens every nontrivial boundary arc. Indeed, subdivide such an arc at its endpoints and at the finitely many boundary vertices of the quotient triangulation. Each resulting subarc lies in a boundary edge on which \(F\) has contraction factor \(<1\).

#### 3. The cevian leaf graph

For each quotient triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. The two endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary sides. Glue two such endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary sides are the same edge of the quotient triangulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\). A point of \(\Lambda_M\) will be called a leaf; geometrically it is a single cevian segment in \(M_\beta\).

Each nonboundary edge is an interior edge of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\), so \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

It is connected. If \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, except at the two boundary extremes, so all triangles incident to that boundary vertex belong to the same \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is also closed, because the quotient triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Therefore
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) in the same way using the triangulation \(\mathcal T\) upstairs. The same open-closed argument applies; closedness follows because a locally finite union of closed triangles is closed. Since \(\Sigma\) is connected, \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection \(\Sigma\to M_\beta\) induces a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G^{2n}\) translates both endpoints by \(2n\beta\), so it can fix the leaf only when \(n=0\). An odd power \(G^{2n+1}\) would force simultaneously
\[
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible for \(\beta>0\). Hence the covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, it is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).

Choose a coordinate \(s\in\mathbb R\) on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) such that the deck transformation \(G\) sends \(s\) to \(s+1\). Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate segment, because cevians are strictly lengthened. Let \(L_s\) be the oriented support line of the image segment.

If a leaf has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G\) sends it to a leaf whose bottom endpoint is \(G(t)\) and whose top endpoint is \(G(b)\). Since \(f\circ G=f\), the image segment is the same geometric segment with the opposite orientation. Therefore
\[
L_{s+1}=L_s^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

#### 4. A lemma on paths of oriented lines

**Lemma 2.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then there are two distinct parameters whose underlying unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. The vector \(m\) is independent of the choice of \(p\). Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Extend the path anti-periodically by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution \(I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)\),
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanishes. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. But on the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map with this antipodal half-period property has odd degree: lifting its argument \(\theta(s)\) gives \(\theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi N\) for a fixed integer \(N\), hence degree \(2N+1\). This contradicts the fact that the middle-circle map is homotopic through \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map on \(\delta=0\). Therefore \((a,b)=0\) somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions \(u,v\) are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the lines are nonparallel; \(b=0\) says they are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 2 to the path \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

#### 5. The metric \(T\)-pattern estimate

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), though they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. After possibly interchanging the two leaves, assume \(P\) is not in the relative interior of \(F(B)\). Then the segment \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). Since \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in a ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) connects \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and by Lemma 1 its image is strictly longer. Hence
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\); otherwise \(Q\in\ell_T\), so \(y=0\).

Let \(a,c\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and put
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The endpoints \(a,c\) are distinct on the boundary circle of \(M_\beta\), since otherwise a lift of them would be deck-equivalent and would have equal \(f\)-images, contradicting the strict lengthening of \(T\).

Let \(D\) be the boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Their flat lengths sum to \(2\beta\), so for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\),
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with bottom endpoint \((x,0)\) and top endpoint \((x+h_T,1)\). Identify the boundary of \(M_\beta\) with \(\mathbb R/2\beta\mathbb Z\) by assigning coordinate \(u\) to \((u,0)\) and coordinate \(u-\beta\) to \((u,1)\). The coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is therefore
\[
h_T-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths between them are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), we get
\[
h_T\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), this implies
\[
|h_T|\ge |t|.
\]
Thus, by strict lengthening of \(T\),
\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h_T^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(H\), strict boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
The image \(F(H)\) joins \(A\) to \(C\), so
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Using (1),
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) joins \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the broken-line length is minimized when the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) to \(AC\) is the midpoint of \(AC\), so
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Strict boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

This proves the lower bound.

---

### II. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and a realizing map.

#### 1. A \(9\)-periodic triangulation of the strip

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(L<W\). Define
\[
\sigma=\frac{L+W}{2},\qquad \Delta=\frac{W-L}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\sigma=L+\Delta=W-\Delta,\qquad \Delta>0.
\]
Choose
\[
\lambda=\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{40},\frac{\beta}{24}\right\}.
\]

Define a \(3\)-periodic sequence \((\ell_j)_{j\in\mathbb Z}\) by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Every block of three consecutive \(\ell_j\)’s has sum \(W\), and every block of nine has sum \(3W=2\beta\).

Let \(c_0=0\) and define
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j,\qquad j\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
For each \(j\), let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(\theta\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_j+\theta(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_{j+1}+\theta(c_{j+6}-\beta),
\]
and their difference is
\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)-x_j(\theta)
=(1-\theta)\ell_j+\theta\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus, for each fixed height \(\theta\), the intervals \([x_j(\theta),x_{j+1}(\theta)]\) tile the horizontal line. Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\), the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), and define
\[
D_j=[X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j],
\qquad
E_j=[Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}].
\]
Then
\[
\mathcal T=\{D_j,E_j:j\in\mathbb Z\}
\]
is a clean locally finite triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Now
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(Y_{j+4}=(c_{j+9}-\beta,1)=(c_j+\beta,1)\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So \(\mathcal T\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient \(M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), let \(v_j\) be the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), the indices \(j\) are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has nine triangular faces
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

#### 2. The target vertices

Choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\subset\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). Let
\[
R=|p_i-o|=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3}.
\]
Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). Choose
\[
\eta=\frac12\min\left\{\frac12,\frac{\lambda}{8R},\frac{\Delta}{16R}\right\},
\qquad
h=\frac12\min\left\{\frac{\lambda}{8},\frac{\Delta}{16}\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
\eta R\le\frac{\lambda}{16},\qquad h\le\frac{\lambda}{16},
\]
and
\[
\eta R\le\frac{\Delta}{32},\qquad h\le\frac{\Delta}{32}.
\]

For \(r>0\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define nine target vertices \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) by
\[
(P_0,\dots,P_8)
=
(C_0,M_0,A_0,A_1,C_1,M_1,M_2,A_2,C_2),
\]
and extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map
\[
\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3
\]
by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j
\]
and extending affinely over each face
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}].
\]
The nine image faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\mathcal F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
\mathcal F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
\mathcal F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
\mathcal F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\mathcal F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\mathcal F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\mathcal F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
\mathcal F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

#### 3. Embeddedness of the target Möbius band

Let
\[
H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}
\]
be the horizontal triangle of scale \(r\) at height \(z\), and let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) be the side of \(H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\).

Use barycentric coordinates \((\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\) with respect to the original equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\) in each horizontal plane. With
\[
a(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]
we have
\[
H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge a(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\},
\]
and if \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=a(r)\}\cap H(r,z).
\]
It follows that adjacent side segments \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only at their common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The three horizontal triangular faces are
\[
\mathcal F_2=H(1,0),\qquad
\mathcal F_5=H(1-\eta,h),\qquad
\mathcal F_8=H(1+\eta,2h).
\]
The remaining six faces form three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
\Pi_{01}=\mathcal F_0\cup\mathcal F_4,\qquad
\Pi_{12}=\mathcal F_3\cup\mathcal F_7,\qquad
\Pi_{20}=\mathcal F_1\cup\mathcal F_6.
\]
Their horizontal slices are
\[
\Pi_{01}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad h\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
\[
\Pi_{12}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
and
\[
\Pi_{20}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le h.
\]

For \(0<\zeta<h\), only \(\Pi_{12}\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
\[
1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}>1,\qquad
1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h}<1,
\]
so the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<\zeta<2h\), only \(\Pi_{01}\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) occur. Their scales satisfy
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac{\zeta}{h}\right)>0,
\]
so again the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(\zeta=0\), the triangle \(H(1,0)\) meets the panels exactly along the intended sides \(A_1A_2\) and \(A_2A_0\). At \(\zeta=h\), the triangle \(H(1-\eta,h)\) meets the panels exactly along \(M_0M_1\) and \(M_2M_0\); the extra slice of \(\Pi_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), larger than \(1-\eta\), and is disjoint from the middle triangle and from the other two side slices. At \(\zeta=2h\), the triangle \(H(1+\eta,2h)\) meets the panels exactly along \(C_0C_1\) and \(C_1C_2\).

Each \(\Pi_{ij}\) is a convex planar trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles inside a panel meet only along that diagonal. The slice analysis shows that distinct panels and horizontal triangles have no unintended intersections. Thus the nine image triangles meet exactly according to the quotient complex. Hence \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

#### 4. The squeeze inequalities

Let the three clusters be
\[
\mathcal C_i=\{A_i,M_i,C_i\},\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]
If \(U_i,V_i\in\mathcal C_i\), then
\[
|U_iV_i|\le 2\eta R+2h\le\frac{\lambda}{4}<\frac{\lambda}{2}.
\]
If \(i\ne k\), \(U_i\in\mathcal C_i\), and \(V_k\in\mathcal C_k\), then \(U_iV_k\) differs from the original equilateral edge \(p_ip_k\) by a perturbation of norm at most \(2\eta R+2h\le\Delta/8\). Therefore
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}<|U_iV_k|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}. \tag{4}
\]

The cluster labels of \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) are
\[
0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2.
\]
Thus the boundary edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) lies inside one cluster when \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), and joins two different clusters when \(j\equiv2\pmod3\).

The flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), while
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\frac{\lambda}{2}<\lambda.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and by (4),
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(W=\sigma+\Delta\) and \(2\lambda\le\Delta/20\),
\[
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
So every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider nonboundary edges. Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
A direct computation from the \(3\)-periodic pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\),
\[
d_j-\beta=
\begin{cases}
-\beta/3+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
\beta/3-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Because \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Thus
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]
The other nonboundary edge in \(D_j\), namely \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), has horizontal displacement
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it also has length \(<L\). These edge types account for all nonboundary edges of the triangles \(D_j,E_j\).

Every target nonboundary edge is of the form \(P_kP_{k+5}\), up to reversing orientation, and therefore joins two different clusters. By (4),
\[
|P_kP_{k+5}|>\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(\sigma=L+\Delta\),
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}=L+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Therefore \(\Phi\) is a squeeze map on every quotient triangle.

#### 5. The lifted realizing map

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with indices on the \(P_j\)’s taken modulo \(9\). Extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous and PL.

Moreover,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) sends triangles affinely to triangles, these vertex equalities imply
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\).

Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\Longleftrightarrow
\Phi([p])=\Phi([q])
\Longleftrightarrow
[p]=[q]
\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
The squeeze inequalities above show that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\). Thus \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized, and the lower bound proved that no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized. Hence the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers and \(\mathbb R\) the reals. For \(\beta>0\), let
\[
\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1],\qquad \partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\},
\]
and let
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).
\]
For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), \(G_\beta^k\) denotes the \(k\)-fold iterate of \(G_\beta\), using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) when \(k<0\), and \(G_\beta^0\) is the identity. A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). Its unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) is called its boundary edge. A clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) is a countable locally finite family of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\). It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the boundary edge and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there are a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) whose restriction to every triangle is a squeeze map and such that, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
The proof below establishes the stronger exact classification:
\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This is not a relaxation of the original problem; it proves the original infimum assertion and identifies the realized set exactly.

## Proof

### I. The lower bound: a realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. Write \(G=G_\beta\). Thus there is a \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) satisfying the two realization properties.

#### 1. Cevian lengthening and a face-to-face refinement

We first record a useful elementary consequence of the squeeze condition.

**Lemma 1.** Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge, and let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For every point \(r=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), \(0\le s\le1\), the cevian \([a,r]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r)|^2-|a-r|^2.
\]
Since the two nonboundary sides are lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave and lies strictly above its chord on \(0<s<1\). Hence \(q(s)>0\) for all \(s\in[0,1]\). The restriction of \(A\) to the boundary edge is an affine contraction by a factor \(<1\), so all nontrivial boundary subsegments are shortened. \(\square\)

We now refine \(\mathcal T_0\). For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every triangulation vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies that only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge, so this produces a countable locally finite refinement. It is still \(G\)-invariant. By Lemma 1, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

Denote the refined triangulation by \(\mathcal T\). It may be taken to be face-to-face: boundary vertex-in-edge incidences and boundary edge overlaps have been subdivided away; transverse crossings of nonboundary edges would force overlap of triangular interiors in a small sector; and two collinear nonboundary edges cannot overlap properly, because a line joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) has unique endpoints on those two boundary lines. Thus distinct closed triangles of \(\mathcal T\) meet, if at all, in a common face.

#### 2. Passage to an embedded finite PL Möbius band

The quotient
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle
\]
is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f(Gp)=f(p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3,\qquad F([p])=f(p).
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition \(p=G^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need a finite regular quotient triangulation. For \(k\ne0\),
\[
G^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),\qquad
G^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y),
\]
so every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\).

If \(f\) were noninjective on some refined triangle \(\Delta\), then the affine linear part of \(f|_\Delta\) would have rank \(<2\), giving arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every closed triangle.

Consequently no closed triangle \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\) meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in\Delta\cap G^k\Delta\), \(k\ne0\), then \(z=G^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\); injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), impossible because the deck action is free.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, and every triangle has some translate meeting it. Therefore \(\mathcal T/\langle G\rangle\) is a finite regular clean triangulation of \(M_\beta\), and \(F\) is affine on each quotient triangle.

Finally, \(F\) strictly shortens every nontrivial boundary arc. Indeed, subdivide such an arc at its endpoints and at the finitely many boundary vertices of the quotient triangulation. Each resulting subarc lies in a boundary edge on which \(F\) has contraction factor \(<1\).

#### 3. The cevian leaf graph

For each quotient triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. The two endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary sides. Glue two such endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary sides are the same edge of the quotient triangulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\). A point of \(\Lambda_M\) will be called a leaf; geometrically it is a single cevian segment in \(M_\beta\).

Each nonboundary edge is an interior edge of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\), so \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

It is connected. If \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, except at the two boundary extremes, so all triangles incident to that boundary vertex belong to the same \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is also closed, because the quotient triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Therefore
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) in the same way using the triangulation \(\mathcal T\) upstairs. The same open-closed argument applies; closedness follows because a locally finite union of closed triangles is closed. Since \(\Sigma\) is connected, \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection \(\Sigma\to M_\beta\) induces a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G^{2n}\) translates both endpoints by \(2n\beta\), so it can fix the leaf only when \(n=0\). An odd power \(G^{2n+1}\) would force simultaneously
\[
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible for \(\beta>0\). Hence the covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, it is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).

Choose a coordinate \(s\in\mathbb R\) on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) such that the deck transformation \(G\) sends \(s\) to \(s+1\). Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate segment, because cevians are strictly lengthened. Let \(L_s\) be the oriented support line of the image segment.

If a leaf has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G\) sends it to a leaf whose bottom endpoint is \(G(t)\) and whose top endpoint is \(G(b)\). Since \(f\circ G=f\), the image segment is the same geometric segment with the opposite orientation. Therefore
\[
L_{s+1}=L_s^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

#### 4. A lemma on paths of oriented lines

**Lemma 2.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then there are two distinct parameters whose underlying unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. The vector \(m\) is independent of the choice of \(p\). Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Extend the path anti-periodically by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution \(I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)\),
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanishes. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. But on the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map with this antipodal half-period property has odd degree: lifting its argument \(\theta(s)\) gives \(\theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi N\) for a fixed integer \(N\), hence degree \(2N+1\). This contradicts the fact that the middle-circle map is homotopic through \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map on \(\delta=0\). Therefore \((a,b)=0\) somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions \(u,v\) are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the lines are nonparallel; \(b=0\) says they are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 2 to the path \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

#### 5. The metric \(T\)-pattern estimate

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), though they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. After possibly interchanging the two leaves, assume \(P\) is not in the relative interior of \(F(B)\). Then the segment \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). Since \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in a ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) connects \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and by Lemma 1 its image is strictly longer. Hence
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\); otherwise \(Q\in\ell_T\), so \(y=0\).

Let \(a,c\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and put
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The endpoints \(a,c\) are distinct on the boundary circle of \(M_\beta\), since otherwise a lift of them would be deck-equivalent and would have equal \(f\)-images, contradicting the strict lengthening of \(T\).

Let \(D\) be the boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Their flat lengths sum to \(2\beta\), so for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\),
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with bottom endpoint \((x,0)\) and top endpoint \((x+h_T,1)\). Identify the boundary of \(M_\beta\) with \(\mathbb R/2\beta\mathbb Z\) by assigning coordinate \(u\) to \((u,0)\) and coordinate \(u-\beta\) to \((u,1)\). The coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is therefore
\[
h_T-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths between them are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), we get
\[
h_T\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), this implies
\[
|h_T|\ge |t|.
\]
Thus, by strict lengthening of \(T\),
\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h_T^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(H\), strict boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
The image \(F(H)\) joins \(A\) to \(C\), so
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Using (1),
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) joins \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the broken-line length is minimized when the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) to \(AC\) is the midpoint of \(AC\), so
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Strict boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

This proves the lower bound.

---

### II. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and a realizing map.

#### 1. A \(9\)-periodic triangulation of the strip

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(L<W\). Define
\[
\sigma=\frac{L+W}{2},\qquad \Delta=\frac{W-L}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\sigma=L+\Delta=W-\Delta,\qquad \Delta>0.
\]
Choose
\[
\lambda=\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{40},\frac{\beta}{24}\right\}.
\]

Define a \(3\)-periodic sequence \((\ell_j)_{j\in\mathbb Z}\) by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Every block of three consecutive \(\ell_j\)’s has sum \(W\), and every block of nine has sum \(3W=2\beta\).

Let \(c_0=0\) and define
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j,\qquad j\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
For each \(j\), let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(\theta\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_j+\theta(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_{j+1}+\theta(c_{j+6}-\beta),
\]
and their difference is
\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)-x_j(\theta)
=(1-\theta)\ell_j+\theta\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus, for each fixed height \(\theta\), the intervals \([x_j(\theta),x_{j+1}(\theta)]\) tile the horizontal line. Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\), the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), and define
\[
D_j=[X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j],
\qquad
E_j=[Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}].
\]
Then
\[
\mathcal T=\{D_j,E_j:j\in\mathbb Z\}
\]
is a clean locally finite triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Now
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(Y_{j+4}=(c_{j+9}-\beta,1)=(c_j+\beta,1)\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So \(\mathcal T\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient \(M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), let \(v_j\) be the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), the indices \(j\) are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has nine triangular faces
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

#### 2. The target vertices

Choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\subset\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). Let
\[
R=|p_i-o|=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3}.
\]
Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). Choose
\[
\eta=\frac12\min\left\{\frac12,\frac{\lambda}{8R},\frac{\Delta}{16R}\right\},
\qquad
h=\frac12\min\left\{\frac{\lambda}{8},\frac{\Delta}{16}\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
\eta R\le\frac{\lambda}{16},\qquad h\le\frac{\lambda}{16},
\]
and
\[
\eta R\le\frac{\Delta}{32},\qquad h\le\frac{\Delta}{32}.
\]

For \(r>0\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define nine target vertices \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) by
\[
(P_0,\dots,P_8)
=
(C_0,M_0,A_0,A_1,C_1,M_1,M_2,A_2,C_2),
\]
and extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map
\[
\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3
\]
by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j
\]
and extending affinely over each face
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}].
\]
The nine image faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\mathcal F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
\mathcal F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
\mathcal F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
\mathcal F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\mathcal F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\mathcal F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\mathcal F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
\mathcal F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

#### 3. Embeddedness of the target Möbius band

Let
\[
H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}
\]
be the horizontal triangle of scale \(r\) at height \(z\), and let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) be the side of \(H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\).

Use barycentric coordinates \((\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\) with respect to the original equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\) in each horizontal plane. With
\[
a(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]
we have
\[
H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge a(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\},
\]
and if \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=a(r)\}\cap H(r,z).
\]
It follows that adjacent side segments \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only at their common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The three horizontal triangular faces are
\[
\mathcal F_2=H(1,0),\qquad
\mathcal F_5=H(1-\eta,h),\qquad
\mathcal F_8=H(1+\eta,2h).
\]
The remaining six faces form three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
\Pi_{01}=\mathcal F_0\cup\mathcal F_4,\qquad
\Pi_{12}=\mathcal F_3\cup\mathcal F_7,\qquad
\Pi_{20}=\mathcal F_1\cup\mathcal F_6.
\]
Their horizontal slices are
\[
\Pi_{01}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad h\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
\[
\Pi_{12}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
and
\[
\Pi_{20}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le h.
\]

For \(0<\zeta<h\), only \(\Pi_{12}\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
\[
1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}>1,\qquad
1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h}<1,
\]
so the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<\zeta<2h\), only \(\Pi_{01}\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) occur. Their scales satisfy
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac{\zeta}{h}\right)>0,
\]
so again the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(\zeta=0\), the triangle \(H(1,0)\) meets the panels exactly along the intended sides \(A_1A_2\) and \(A_2A_0\). At \(\zeta=h\), the triangle \(H(1-\eta,h)\) meets the panels exactly along \(M_0M_1\) and \(M_2M_0\); the extra slice of \(\Pi_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), larger than \(1-\eta\), and is disjoint from the middle triangle and from the other two side slices. At \(\zeta=2h\), the triangle \(H(1+\eta,2h)\) meets the panels exactly along \(C_0C_1\) and \(C_1C_2\).

Each \(\Pi_{ij}\) is a convex planar trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles inside a panel meet only along that diagonal. The slice analysis shows that distinct panels and horizontal triangles have no unintended intersections. Thus the nine image triangles meet exactly according to the quotient complex. Hence \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

#### 4. The squeeze inequalities

Let the three clusters be
\[
\mathcal C_i=\{A_i,M_i,C_i\},\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]
If \(U_i,V_i\in\mathcal C_i\), then
\[
|U_iV_i|\le 2\eta R+2h\le\frac{\lambda}{4}<\frac{\lambda}{2}.
\]
If \(i\ne k\), \(U_i\in\mathcal C_i\), and \(V_k\in\mathcal C_k\), then \(U_iV_k\) differs from the original equilateral edge \(p_ip_k\) by a perturbation of norm at most \(2\eta R+2h\le\Delta/8\). Therefore
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}<|U_iV_k|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}. \tag{4}
\]

The cluster labels of \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) are
\[
0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2.
\]
Thus the boundary edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) lies inside one cluster when \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), and joins two different clusters when \(j\equiv2\pmod3\).

The flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), while
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\frac{\lambda}{2}<\lambda.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and by (4),
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(W=\sigma+\Delta\) and \(2\lambda\le\Delta/20\),
\[
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
So every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider nonboundary edges. Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
A direct computation from the \(3\)-periodic pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\),
\[
d_j-\beta=
\begin{cases}
-\beta/3+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
\beta/3-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Because \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Thus
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]
The other nonboundary edge in \(D_j\), namely \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), has horizontal displacement
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it also has length \(<L\). These edge types account for all nonboundary edges of the triangles \(D_j,E_j\).

Every target nonboundary edge is of the form \(P_kP_{k+5}\), up to reversing orientation, and therefore joins two different clusters. By (4),
\[
|P_kP_{k+5}|>\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(\sigma=L+\Delta\),
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}=L+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Therefore \(\Phi\) is a squeeze map on every quotient triangle.

#### 5. The lifted realizing map

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with indices on the \(P_j\)’s taken modulo \(9\). Extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous and PL.

Moreover,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) sends triangles affinely to triangles, these vertex equalities imply
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\).

Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\Longleftrightarrow
\Phi([p])=\Phi([q])
\Longleftrightarrow
[p]=[q]
\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
The squeeze inequalities above show that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\). Thus \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized, and the lower bound proved that no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized. Hence the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)
self_declared_problem_solved: For every real beta>0, with Sigma=R x [0,1], boundary dSigma=R x {0,1}, G_beta(x,y)=(x+beta,1-y), clean triangles, clean triangulations, squeeze maps, and realized beta defined exactly as in the original problem, beta is realized if and only if beta>sqrt(3). Consequently the set of realized numbers is exactly (sqrt(3), infinity), and its infimum is sqrt(3).
advisor_statement: The realized numbers β > 0 are exactly the interval (√3, ∞), and therefore the infimum of the set of realized numbers is √3.

Consequently the set of realized numbers is exactly (sqrt(3), infinity), and its infimum is sqrt(3).
